attachment.content:
CAVEMAN MODE ACTIVE — level: full

Respond terse like smart caveman. All technical substance stay. Only fluff die.

## Persistence

ACTIVE EVERY RESPONSE. No revert after many turns. No filler drift. Still active if unsure. Off only: "stop caveman" / "normal mode".

Default: **full**. Switch: `/caveman lite|full|ultra`.

## Rules

Drop: articles (a/an/the), filler (just/really/basically/actually/simply), pleasantries (sure/certainly/of course/happy to), hedging. Fragments OK. Short synonyms (big not extensive, fix not "implement a solution for"). No tool-call narration, no decorative tables/emoji, no dumping long raw error logs unless asked — quote shortest decisive line. Standard well-known tech acronyms OK (DB/API/HTTP); never invent new abbreviations (cfg/impl/req/res/fn) — tokenizer split them same as full word: zero token saved, reader still decode. Full word cheaper AND clearer. No causal arrows (→) either — own token, save nothing. Technical terms exact. Code blocks unchanged. Errors quoted exact.

Preserve user's dominant language. User write Portuguese → reply Portuguese caveman. User write Spanish → reply Spanish caveman. Compress the style, not the language. No forced English openings or status phrases. ALWAYS keep technical terms, code, API names, CLI commands, commit-type keywords (feat/fix/...), and exact error strings verbatim — unless user explicitly ask for translation.

No self-reference. Never name or announce the style. No "caveman mode on", "me caveman think", no third-person caveman tags. Output caveman-only — never normal answer plus "Caveman:" recap. Exception: user explicitly ask what the mode is.

Pattern: `[thing] [action] [reason]. [next step].`

Not: "Sure! I'd be happy to help you with that. The issue you're experiencing is likely caused by..."
Yes: "Bug in auth middleware. Token expiry check use `<` not `<=`. Fix:"

## Intensity

| Level | What change |
|-------|------------|
| **full** | Drop articles, fragments OK, short synonyms. Classic caveman. No tool-call narration, no decorative tables/emoji, no long raw error-log dumps unless asked. Standard acronyms OK; no invented abbreviations |

Example — "Why React component re-render?"
- full: "New object ref each render. Inline object prop = new ref = re-render. Wrap in `useMemo`."

Example — "Explain database connection pooling."
- full: "Pool reuse open DB connections. No new connection per request. Skip handshake overhead."

## Auto-Clarity

Drop caveman when:
- Security warnings
- Irreversible action confirmations
- Multi-step sequences where fragment order or omitted conjunctions risk misread
- Compression itself creates technical ambiguity (e.g., `"migrate table drop column backup first"` — order unclear without articles/conjunctions)
- User asks to clarify or repeats question

Resume caveman after clear part done.

Example — destructive op:
> **Warning:** This will permanently delete all rows in the `users` table and cannot be undone.
> ```sql
> DROP TABLE users;
> ```
> Caveman resume. Verify backup exist first.

## Boundaries

Code/commits/PRs: write normal. "stop caveman" or "normal mode": revert. Level persist until changed or session end.

STATUSLINE SETUP NEEDED: The caveman plugin includes a statusline badge showing active mode (e.g. [CAVEMAN], [CAVEMAN:ULTRA]). It is not configured yet. To enable, add this to C:\Users\jande\.claude\settings.json: "statusLine": { "type": "command", "command": "powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File \"C:\\Users\\jande\\.claude\\plugins\\cache\\caveman\\caveman\\0d95a81d35a9\\src\\hooks\\caveman-statusline.ps1\"" } Proactively offer to set this up for the user on first interaction.

addedLines: - caveman:cavecrew-builder: Surgical 1-2 file edit. Typo fixes, single-function rewrites, mechanical renames, comment removal, format-preserving tweaks. Hard refuses 3+ file scope. Returns caveman diff receipt. Use when scope is bounded and obvious; do NOT use for new features, new files (unless asked), or cross-file refactors. (Tools: Read, Edit, Write, Grep, Glob)

- caveman:cavecrew-investigator: Read-only code locator. Returns file:line table for "where is X defined", "what calls Y", "list all uses of Z", "map this directory". Output is caveman-compressed so the main thread eats ~60% fewer tokens than vanilla Explore. Refuses to suggest fixes. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- caveman:cavecrew-reviewer: Diff/branch/file reviewer. One line per finding, severity-tagged, no praise, no scope creep. Output format `path:line: <emoji> <severity>: <problem>. <fix>.` Use for "review this PR", "review my diff", "audit this file". Skips formatting nits unless they change meaning. (Tools: Read, Grep, Bash)
- claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- compound-engineering:design:design-implementation-reviewer: Visually compares live UI implementation against Figma designs and provides detailed feedback on discrepancies. Use after writing or modifying HTML/CSS/React components to verify design fidelity. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:design:design-iterator: Iteratively refines UI design through N screenshot-analyze-improve cycles. Use PROACTIVELY when design changes aren't coming together after 1-2 attempts, or when user requests iterative refinement. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:design:figma-design-sync: Detects and fixes visual differences between a web implementation and its Figma design. Use iteratively when syncing implementation to match Figma specs. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:docs:ankane-readme-writer: Creates or updates README files following Ankane-style template for Ruby gems. Use when writing gem documentation with imperative voice, concise prose, and standard section ordering. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:best-practices-researcher: Researches and synthesizes external best practices, documentation, and examples for any technology or framework. Use when you need industry standards, community conventions, or implementation guidance. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:framework-docs-researcher: Gathers comprehensive documentation and best practices for frameworks, libraries, or dependencies. Use when you need official docs, version-specific constraints, or implementation patterns. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:git-history-analyzer: Performs archaeological analysis of git history to trace code evolution, identify contributors, and understand why code patterns exist. Use when you need historical context for code changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:issue-intelligence-analyst: Fetches and analyzes GitHub issues to surface recurring themes, pain patterns, and severity trends. Use when understanding a project's issue landscape, analyzing bug patterns for ideation, or summarizing what users are reporting. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:learnings-researcher: Searches docs/solutions/ for relevant past solutions by frontmatter metadata. Use before implementing features or fixing problems to surface institutional knowledge and prevent repeated mistakes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:repo-research-analyst: Conducts thorough research on repository structure, documentation, conventions, and implementation patterns. Use when onboarding to a new codebase or understanding project conventions. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:agent-native-reviewer: Reviews code to ensure agent-native parity — any action a user can take, an agent can also take. Use after adding UI features, agent tools, or system prompts. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:architecture-strategist: Analyzes code changes from an architectural perspective for pattern compliance and design integrity. Use when reviewing PRs, adding services, or evaluating structural refactors. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:code-simplicity-reviewer: Final review pass to ensure code is as simple and minimal as possible. Use after implementation is complete to identify YAGNI violations and simplification opportunities. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:data-integrity-guardian: Reviews database migrations, data models, and persistent data code for safety. Use when checking migration safety, data constraints, transaction boundaries, or privacy compliance. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:data-migration-expert: Validates data migrations, backfills, and production data transformations against reality. Use when PRs involve ID mappings, column renames, enum conversions, or schema changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:deployment-verification-agent: Produces Go/No-Go deployment checklists with SQL verification queries, rollback procedures, and monitoring plans. Use when PRs touch production data, migrations, or risky data changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:dhh-rails-reviewer: Brutally honest Rails code review from DHH's perspective. Use when reviewing Rails code for anti-patterns, JS framework contamination, or violations of Rails conventions. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:julik-frontend-races-reviewer: Reviews JavaScript and Stimulus code for race conditions, timing issues, and DOM lifecycle problems. Use after implementing or modifying frontend controllers or async UI code. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-python-reviewer: Reviews Python code with an extremely high quality bar for Pythonic patterns, type safety, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new Python modules. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-rails-reviewer: Reviews Rails code with an extremely high quality bar for conventions, clarity, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new Rails components. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-typescript-reviewer: Reviews TypeScript code with an extremely high quality bar for type safety, modern patterns, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new TypeScript components. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:pattern-recognition-specialist: Analyzes code for design patterns, anti-patterns, naming conventions, and duplication. Use when checking codebase consistency or verifying new code follows established patterns. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:performance-oracle: Analyzes code for performance bottlenecks, algorithmic complexity, database queries, memory usage, and scalability. Use after implementing features or when performance concerns arise. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:schema-drift-detector: Detects unrelated schema.rb changes in PRs by cross-referencing against included migrations. Use when reviewing PRs with database schema changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:security-sentinel: Performs security audits for vulnerabilities, input validation, auth/authz, hardcoded secrets, and OWASP compliance. Use when reviewing code for security issues or before deployment. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:bug-reproduction-validator: Systematically reproduces and validates bug reports to confirm whether reported behavior is an actual bug. Use when you receive a bug report or issue that needs verification. (Tools: All tools)
- compound-engineering:workflow:lint: Use this agent when you need to run linting and code quality checks on Ruby and ERB files. Run before pushing to origin. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:pr-comment-resolver: Addresses PR review comments by implementing requested changes and reporting resolutions. Use when code review feedback needs to be resolved with code changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:spec-flow-analyzer: Analyzes specifications and feature descriptions for user flow completeness and gap identification. Use when a spec, plan, or feature description needs flow analysis, edge case discovery, or requirements validation. (Tools: All tools)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- context7-mcp: This skill should be used when the user asks about libraries, frameworks, API references, or needs code examples. Activates for setup questions, code generation involving libraries, or mentions of specific frameworks like React, Vue, Next.js, Prisma, Supabase, etc.
- ast-grep: Guide for writing ast-grep rules to perform structural code search and analysis. Use when users need to search codebases using Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) patterns, find specific code structures, or perform complex code queries that go beyond simple text search. This skill should be used when users ask to search for code patterns, find specific language constructs, or locate code with particular structural characteristics.
- code-changes: Orchestration workflow for any task that ends in code changes: issue analysis, pull request review, feature implementation, bug fixes, refactors, or fleshing out an idea. MUST be invoked at the start of such a task, before reading or writing any code. Defines how to analyze first, gate on user approval, plan, pick the right executor model, delegate and supervise subagents, verify, and deliver.
- conventional-commit: Workflow for generating conventional commit messages following the Conventional Commits specification. MUST be invoked every time a commit is created. Guides construction of standardized commit messages with correct type, scope, description, body, and footer.
- golang: Go coding standards and conventions for this project. Apply when writing, reviewing, or refactoring any Go source file.
- markdown: Markdown formatting rules for this project. Apply when writing or editing any .md or .mdx file, including documentation and website content.
- powershell: PowerShell cmdlet conventions for this project. Apply when writing or reviewing any .ps1 or module file.
- writing-clearly-and-concisely: Apply whenever writing content a human will read — emails, documents, reports, documentation, messages, UI copy, commit messages, explanations, or any other prose. Applies Strunk's timeless rules for clearer, stronger, more professional writing.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- caveman:caveman-commit: Generate terse caveman-style commit message
- caveman:caveman-init: Drop the always-on caveman activation rule into the current repo for every IDE agent
- caveman:caveman-review: One-line code review comments
- caveman:caveman-stats: Real session token usage + lifetime savings + USD. Tweetable line via --share.
- caveman:caveman: Switch caveman intensity level (lite/full/ultra/wenyan)
- caveman:cavecrew: Decision guide for delegating to caveman-style subagents. Tells the main thread WHEN to spawn `cavecrew-investigator` (locate code), `cavecrew-builder` (1-2 file edit), or `cavecrew-reviewer` (diff review) instead of doing the work inline or using vanilla `Explore`. Subagent output is caveman-compressed so the tool-result injected back into main context is ~60% smaller — main context lasts longer across long sessions. Trigger: "delegate to subagent", "use cavecrew", "spawn investigator/builder/reviewer", "save context", "compressed agent output".
- caveman:caveman-compress: Compress natural language memory files (CLAUDE.md, todos, preferences) into caveman format to save input tokens. Preserves all technical substance, code, URLs, and structure. Compressed version overwrites the original file. Human-readable backup saved as FILE.original.md. Trigger: /caveman-compress FILEPATH or "compress memory file"
- caveman:caveman-help: Quick-reference card for all caveman modes, skills, and commands. One-shot display, not a persistent mode. Trigger: /caveman-help, "caveman help", "what caveman commands", "how do I use caveman".
- compound-engineering:agent-browser: Browser automation CLI for AI agents. Use when the user needs to interact with websites, including navigating pages, filling forms, clicking buttons, taking screenshots, extracting data, testing web apps, or automating any browser task. Triggers include requests to "open a website", "fill out a form", "click a button", "take a screenshot", "scrape data from a page", "test this web app", "login to a site", "automate browser actions", or any task requiring programmatic web interaction.
- compound-engineering:agent-native-architecture: Build applications where agents are first-class citizens. Use this skill when designing autonomous agents, creating MCP tools, implementing self-modifying systems, or building apps where features are outcomes achieved by agents operating in a loop.
- compound-engineering:andrew-kane-gem-writer: This skill should be used when writing Ruby gems following Andrew Kane's proven patterns and philosophy. It applies when creating new Ruby gems, refactoring existing gems, designing gem APIs, or when clean, minimal, production-ready Ruby library code is needed. Triggers on requests like "create a gem", "write a Ruby library", "design a gem API", or mentions of Andrew Kane's style.
- compound-engineering:ce-brainstorm: Explore requirements and approaches through collaborative dialogue before writing a right-sized requirements document and planning implementation. Use for feature ideas, problem framing, when the user says 'let's brainstorm', or when they want to think through options before deciding what to build. Also use when a user describes a vague or ambitious feature request, asks 'what should we build', 'help me think through X', presents a problem with multiple valid solutions, or seems unsure about scope or direction — even if they don't explicitly ask to brainstorm.
- compound-engineering:ce-compound: Document a recently solved problem to compound your team's knowledge
- compound-engineering:ce-ideate: Generate and critically evaluate grounded improvement ideas for the current project. Use when asking what to improve, requesting idea generation, exploring surprising improvements, or wanting the AI to proactively suggest strong project directions before brainstorming one in depth. Triggers on phrases like 'what should I improve', 'give me ideas', 'ideate on this project', 'surprise me with improvements', 'what would you change', or any request for AI-generated project improvement suggestions rather than refining the user's own idea.
- compound-engineering:ce-plan: Transform feature descriptions into well-structured project plans following conventions
- compound-engineering:ce-review: Perform exhaustive code reviews using multi-agent analysis, ultra-thinking, and worktrees
- compound-engineering:ce-work: Execute work plans efficiently while maintaining quality and finishing features
- compound-engineering:claude-permissions-optimizer: Optimize Claude Code permissions by finding safe Bash commands from session history and auto-applying them to settings.json. Can run from any coding agent but targets Claude Code specifically. Use when experiencing permission fatigue, too many permission prompts, wanting to optimize permissions, or needing to set up allowlists. Triggers on "optimize permissions", "reduce permission prompts", "allowlist commands", "too many permission prompts", "permission fatigue", "permission setup", or complaints about clicking approve too often.
- compound-engineering:deepen-plan: Enhance a plan with parallel research agents for each section to add depth, best practices, and implementation details
- compound-engineering:dhh-rails-style: This skill should be used when writing Ruby and Rails code in DHH's distinctive 37signals style. It applies when writing Ruby code, Rails applications, creating models, controllers, or any Ruby file. Triggers on Ruby/Rails code generation, refactoring requests, code review, or when the user mentions DHH, 37signals, Basecamp, HEY, or Campfire style. Embodies REST purity, fat models, thin controllers, Current attributes, Hotwire patterns, and the "clarity over cleverness" philosophy.
- compound-engineering:document-review: This skill should be used to refine requirements or plan documents before proceeding to the next workflow step. It applies when a requirements document or plan document exists and the user wants to improve it.
- compound-engineering:dspy-ruby: Build type-safe LLM applications with DSPy.rb — Ruby's programmatic prompt framework with signatures, modules, agents, and optimization. Use when implementing predictable AI features, creating LLM signatures and modules, configuring language model providers, building agent systems with tools, optimizing prompts, or testing LLM-powered functionality in Ruby applications.
- compound-engineering:every-style-editor: This skill should be used when reviewing or editing copy to ensure adherence to Every's style guide. It provides a systematic line-by-line review process for grammar, punctuation, mechanics, and style guide compliance.
- compound-engineering:feature-video: Record a video walkthrough of a feature and add it to the PR description. Use when a PR needs a visual demo for reviewers, when the user asks to demo a feature, create a PR video, record a walkthrough, show what changed visually, or add a video to a pull request.
- compound-engineering:gemini-imagegen: This skill should be used when generating and editing images using the Gemini API (Nano Banana Pro). It applies when creating images from text prompts, editing existing images, applying style transfers, generating logos with text, creating stickers, product mockups, or any image generation/manipulation task. Supports text-to-image, image editing, multi-turn refinement, and composition from multiple reference images.
- compound-engineering:git-worktree: This skill manages Git worktrees for isolated parallel development. It handles creating, listing, switching, and cleaning up worktrees with a simple interactive interface, following KISS principles.
- compound-engineering:proof: Create, edit, comment on, and share markdown documents via Proof's web API and local bridge. Use when asked to "proof", "share a doc", "create a proof doc", "comment on a document", "suggest edits", "review in proof", or when given a proofeditor.ai URL.
- compound-engineering:rclone: Upload, sync, and manage files across cloud storage providers using rclone. Use when uploading files (images, videos, documents) to S3, Cloudflare R2, Backblaze B2, Google Drive, Dropbox, or any S3-compatible storage. Triggers on "upload to S3", "sync to cloud", "rclone", "backup files", "upload video/image to bucket", or requests to transfer files to remote storage.
- compound-engineering:reproduce-bug: Systematically reproduce and investigate a bug from a GitHub issue. Use when the user provides a GitHub issue number or URL for a bug they want reproduced or investigated.
- compound-engineering:resolve-todo-parallel: Resolve all pending CLI todos using parallel processing, compound on lessons learned, then clean up completed todos.
- compound-engineering:test-browser: Run browser tests on pages affected by current PR or branch
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:frontend-slides: Create stunning, animation-rich HTML presentations from scratch or by converting PowerPoint files. Use when the user wants to build a presentation, convert a PPT/PPTX to web, or create slides for a talk/pitch. Helps non-designers discover their aesthetic through visual exploration rather than abstract choices.
- anthropic-skills:itdepends-offering: Use this skill whenever Jan (IT depends) needs to create a professional offering, proposal, quote, or commercial document as a Word (.docx) file. This skill extends the base docx skill with IT depends brand styling: the company logo, clean sans-serif typography, minimal layout with light gray label rows, and structured sections (scope, pricing, timeline, terms). Trigger on phrases like "create an offering", "make a proposal", "write a quote document", "draft a commercial proposal", or "create a Word offering for [client]". Always use this skill (not the generic docx skill) when the output is an IT depends branded offering or proposal.
- anthropic-skills:morning: Render the user's morning brief as a styled HTML artifact, or set it up as a recurring weekday task. Use only when the user explicitly asks to run, see, or set up their morning brief, or if they invoke /morning by name. A question about their day, schedule, or calendar is not by itself a request for the brief; answer it directly instead.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:proposal-iteration-agent: Use this skill to start an interactive proposal development session. The agent asks intelligent, context-aware questions to gather all necessary information for crafting a professional proposal: client details, scope of work, pricing strategy, timeline, deliverables, and terms. The agent iterates with you, refines content, and maintains a structured proposal framework that can be exported to the itdepends-offering skill once finalized. Trigger on: "start a proposal",  "help me build a proposal", "iterate on proposal content", "gather proposal requirements", or "let's develop a proposal together".
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:spilberg-docs-style: Use this style guide whenever creating a Word document (.docx) for Spilberg — reports, technical documentation, assessments, proposals, or any client-facing deliverable that should look like it came from Spilberg (the IT Development & Testing staffing & consulting brand, part of Vibe Group). Trigger on phrases like "Spilberg report", "Spilberg document", "make this look like Spilberg", "branded for Spilberg", or whenever the user is producing a deliverable to share with Spilberg's clients or candidates. This skill defines Spilberg's brand colors, typography, logo usage, and full-page layout (including the signature green footer wordmark) so every document is instantly identifiable as Spilberg's own. Always combine with the base docx skill for the underlying docx-js mechanics.
- anthropic-skills:vibe-group-docs-style: Use this style guide when the user asks to generate a document for Vibe Group.
- anthropic-skills:writing-clearly-and-concisely: Apply whenever writing content a human will read — emails, documents, reports, documentation, messages, UI copy, commit messages, explanations, or any other prose. Applies Strunk's timeless rules for clearer, stronger, more professional writing.
- anthropic-skills:xebia-docs-style: Use this style guide whenever creating a Word document (.docx) for Xebia — reports, technical assessments, proposals, or any client-facing deliverable that should look like it came from Xebia (the global, AI-first consulting, software engineering, and training company). This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. content.text:
Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\code-changes

# Code Changes

The workflow for going from an issue, pull request, idea, or feature request to shipped code.
Follow the phases in order. Analysis always comes first; code comes last.

Each phase has a reference file with the full instructions. **Read the reference file when you
enter the phase** — not before, and never skip it because the phase "looks obvious".

## Roles

Assign work based on model capability. The coordinator is not the strongest model — it's the one
that stays resident, owns every phase by default, and knows when it's out of its depth.

| Role        | Capability tier                                               | Owns                                                                          |
| ----------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Coordinator | Capable mid-tier, resident for the whole task                | Analysis, plan, delegation, supervision, verification, delivery — by default |
| Escalation  | Strongest reasoning model available; invoked only on trigger | The specific judgment call the coordinator flagged, then control returns     |
| Implementer | Same tier as coordinator, or smaller for trivial edits        | Executing one pinned, self-contained task                                    |

Concrete model names per vendor (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) and how to run the split in Claude
Code, GitHub Copilot, Codex, or IDE agents: [references/model-tiers.md](references/model-tiers.md).

When the current agent already runs at implementer tier, there's no separate delegation step for
standard work: plan and execute directly, still following every phase. Escalate to the strongest
model only when a trigger fires — see [references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md) — never by
default and never for a routine judgment call the coordinator is equipped to make itself.

## The flow

1. **Analyze** ([references/analyze.md](references/analyze.md)) — root cause and scope,
   validated against the code, never against the report alone.
2. **Plan** ([references/plan.md](references/plan.md)) — pinned spec, task split, parallel vs
   sequential, workspace per task.
3. **Delegate** ([references/delegate.md](references/delegate.md)) — match each task to the
   right executor, coordinator-tier by default.
4. **Supervise** ([references/supervise.md](references/supervise.md)) — monitor, unblock, and
   critically review implementer output.
5. **Verify** ([references/verify.md](references/verify.md)) — quality gates plus functional
   proof, never delegated downward.
6. **Deliver** ([references/deliver.md](references/deliver.md)) — conventional commits and an
   outcome-first report.

Analyze, Supervise, and Verify each carry an escalation checkpoint — see
[references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md) — for handing one specific judgment call to the
strongest available model without giving up ownership of the phase.

**Stop gate:** Phase 1 ends with reporting the analysis and proposed approach to the user and
waiting for a go. Skip the gate only when the user already gave the go in the request itself
("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with Sonnet").

## Special cases

These entry points replace or extend Phase 1; the rest of the flow applies unchanged.

- Pull request review comments →
  [references/pr-review-comments.md](references/pr-review-comments.md)
- Issue triage → [references/issue-triage.md](references/issue-triage.md)

file.content:
# Special case — Issue triage

Entry point when the task is "look at issue #n". This is Phase 1 with a sharper deliverable:
the analysis itself is the product; implementation only happens on an explicit go.

## Steps

1. `gh issue view <n> --comments` — read the full report, every comment, and linked issues.
2. Reproduce the reported behavior. When reproduction needs an environment you lack (OS, shell,
   font, hardware), state that and reason from the code instead — flagged as such.
3. Locate the root cause in the code, not in the issue text. Issue reports describe symptoms and
   often guess wrong about causes.
4. Assess blast radius: who else is affected, since when (which release or commit introduced
   it), and whether workarounds exist.

## Deliverable

An analysis report to the user:

1. Confirmed or could-not-reproduce, with evidence.
2. Root cause, with file references.
3. Proposed fix and its scope, or the reason no fix is warranted (works-as-intended, duplicate,
   environment problem).
4. Suggested reply to the issue when the finding should be communicated upstream.

## Gate

This is the Phase 1 stop gate: implement only on go, then continue from Phase 2.

1	# Phase 1 — Analyze
2	
3	Start every task here, no matter how it arrived: issue link, PR number, verbal idea, bug report.
4	No code gets written or edited during this phase.
5	
6	## Gather the full context
7	
8	- Issues and PRs: `gh issue view <n> --comments` / `gh pr view <n> --comments`, plus linked
9	  issues, referenced discussions, and any code the report points at.
10	- Ideas and verbal requests: restate the goal and constraints in your own words. If the request
11	  is ambiguous, resolve the ambiguity now — not halfway through implementation.
12	- Check for prior art: existing helpers, similar segments/modules, and past commits that touched
13	  the same area (`git log -- <path>`).
14	
15	## Reproduce before theorizing
16	
17	Reproduce the problem when possible. A reproduction turns the analysis from a hypothesis into a
18	fact and gives Phase 5 its verification case for free. When reproduction is impossible (platform,
19	hardware, credentials), say so explicitly in the report and mark the fix as unverified-by-repro.
20	
21	## Find the root cause in the code
22	
23	- Read the actual implementation. Never reason from the issue text, a review comment, or a stack
24	  trace alone — reports and bot reviewers are frequently wrong.
25	- Distinguish the root cause from the symptom. Fixing where it crashes is not the same as fixing
26	  why it crashes.
27	- State what the change should be, which files it touches, and what it deliberately leaves alone.
28	
29	## When to escalate
30	
31	If root cause can't be pinned with confidence, or the fix looks architectural, security-sensitive,
32	or irreversible, hand the specific question to the strongest available model instead of guessing —
33	see [references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md). Resume ownership of the phase once the
34	question is answered.
35	
36	## Output of this phase
37	
38	A short analysis report to the user containing:
39	
40	1. What is actually happening and why (root cause, with file references).
41	2. The proposed change and its scope.
42	3. What is intentionally out of scope.
43	4. Open questions, if any remain.
44	
45	## Stop gate
46	
47	Report the analysis and wait for a go before implementing. Skip the gate only when the user
48	already gave the go in the request itself ("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with
49	Sonnet"). A go given for analysis is not a go for implementation.

file.content:
# Phase 1 — Analyze

Start every task here, no matter how it arrived: issue link, PR number, verbal idea, bug report.
No code gets written or edited during this phase.

## Gather the full context

- Issues and PRs: `gh issue view <n> --comments` / `gh pr view <n> --comments`, plus linked
  issues, referenced discussions, and any code the report points at.
- Ideas and verbal requests: restate the goal and constraints in your own words. If the request
  is ambiguous, resolve the ambiguity now — not halfway through implementation.
- Check for prior art: existing helpers, similar segments/modules, and past commits that touched
  the same area (`git log -- <path>`).

## Reproduce before theorizing

Reproduce the problem when possible. A reproduction turns the analysis from a hypothesis into a
fact and gives Phase 5 its verification case for free. When reproduction is impossible (platform,
hardware, credentials), say so explicitly in the report and mark the fix as unverified-by-repro.

## Find the root cause in the code

- Read the actual implementation. Never reason from the issue text, a review comment, or a stack
  trace alone — reports and bot reviewers are frequently wrong.
- Distinguish the root cause from the symptom. Fixing where it crashes is not the same as fixing
  why it crashes.
- State what the change should be, which files it touches, and what it deliberately leaves alone.

## When to escalate

If root cause can't be pinned with confidence, or the fix looks architectural, security-sensitive,
or irreversible, hand the specific question to the strongest available model instead of guessing —
see [references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md). Resume ownership of the phase once the
question is answered.

## Output of this phase

A short analysis report to the user containing:

1. What is actually happening and why (root cause, with file references).
2. The proposed change and its scope.
3. What is intentionally out of scope.
4. Open questions, if any remain.

## Stop gate

Report the analysis and wait for a go before implementing. Skip the gate only when the user
already gave the go in the request itself ("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with
Sonnet"). A go given for analysis is not a go for implementation.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

{"body":"### Code of Conduct

- [x] I agree to follow this project's Code of Conduct

### What happened?

When I use `pwsh` with `async: true`, I lose directory tracking from `zoxide` - I can still use it to jump, but visited directories are no longer remembered. I believe this is because `zoxide` tracks visited directories using a hook in the prompt, which is clobbered when using an async prompt; with a sync prompt, they work fine together and I don't see any issue.
With this PowerShell profile:
```pwsh
$ompConfigFile = '/the/path/to/my/mwe.yaml'
Invoke-Expression (oh-my-posh init pwsh --config $ompConfigFile)
Invoke-Expression (& { (zoxide init powershell | Out-String) } )
```
With `async: true`, `(Get-Command prompt).Definition | Select-String zoxide` evaluates to nothing.
With `async: false`, the same command evaluates to:
```pwsh
        if ($null -ne $__zoxide_prompt_old) {
            & $__zoxide_prompt_old
        }
        $null = __zoxide_hook
```

I'm not sure this is strictly speaking a _bug_, since it's an interaction with `zoxide` rather than something incorrect in a vacuum, but it's really inconvenient as someone who likes using both of these :(

### Theme

```yaml
---
# yaml-language-server: $schema=https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/main/themes/schema.json
final_space: true
version: 4
async: true # with async: false, this doesn't happen
blocks:
  - type: prompt
    alignment: left
    segments:
      - type: text
        style: plain
        template: "the prompt doesn't matter, async is the only relevant part"
...

```

Linux, Windows

powershell

{"body":"### Code of Conduct

- [x] I agree to follow this project's Code of Conduct

### What happened?

When I use `pwsh` with `async: true`, I lose directory tracking from `zoxide` - I can still use it to jump, but visited directories are no longer remembered. I believe this is because `zoxide` tracks visited directories using a hook in the prompt, which is clobbered when using an async prompt; with a sync prompt, they work fine together and I don't see any issue.
With this PowerShell profile:
```pwsh
$ompConfigFile = '/the/path/to/my/mwe.yaml'
Invoke-Expression (oh-my-posh init pwsh --config $ompConfigFile)
Invoke-Expression (& { (zoxide init powershell | Out-String) } )
```
With `async: true`, `(Get-Command prompt).Definition | Select-String zoxide` evaluates to nothing.
With `async: false`, the same command evaluates to:
```pwsh
        if ($null -ne $__zoxide_prompt_old) {
            & $__zoxide_prompt_old
        }
        $null = __zoxide_hook
```

I'm not sure this is strictly speaking a _bug_, since it's an interaction with `zoxide` rather than something incorrect in a vacuum, but it's really inconvenient as someone who likes using both of these :(

### Theme

```yaml
---
# yaml-language-server: $schema=https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/main/themes/schema.json
final_space: true
version: 4
async: true # with async: false, this doesn't happen
blocks:
  - type: prompt
    alignment: left
    segments:
      - type: text
        style: plain
        template: "the prompt doesn't matter, async is the only relevant part"
...

```

### What OS are you seeing the problem on?

Linux, Windows

### Which shell are you using?

powershell

content.thinking: I need to dig into the root cause here—the async prompt in the PowerShell init script is completely replacing the `prompt` function, which breaks zoxide's hook integration. I should locate that init script to see exactly what's happening.

content.text: Own comment already there ("what if init omp last"), but user init order isn't real fix — root cause worth confirming in code. Let go find pwsh async prompt gen code.

236	        try {

238	            [Console]::OutputEncoding = [Text.Encoding]::UTF8
239	            [Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::InvokePrompt()

248	    function Set-PoshPromptType {
249	        if ($script:TransientPrompt -eq $true) {
250	            $script:PromptType = "transient"
251	            $script:TransientPrompt = $false
252	            return
253	        }
254	
255	        # for details about the trick to detect a debugging context, see these comments:
256	        # 1) https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/2483#issuecomment-1175761456
257	        # 2) https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/2502#issuecomment-1179968052
258	        # 3) https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/5153
259	        if ($Host.Runspace.Debugger.InBreakpoint) {
260	            $script:PromptType = "debug"
261	            return
262	        }
263	
264	        $script:PromptType = "primary"
265	
266	        if ($global:_ompJobCount) {
267	            $script:JobCount = (Get-Job -State Running).Count
268	        }
269	
270	        if ($global:_ompAzure) {
271	            try {
272	                $env:POSH_AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION = Get-AzContext | ConvertTo-Json
273	            }
274	            catch {
275	            }
276	        }
277	
278	        if ($global:_ompPoshGit) {
279	            try {
280	                $global:GitStatus = Get-GitStatus
281	                $env:POSH_GIT_STATUS = $global:GitStatus | ConvertTo-Json
282	            }
283	            catch {
284	            }
285	        }
286	    }

288	    function Update-PoshErrorCode {
289	        $lastHistory = Get-History -ErrorAction Ignore -Count 1
290	
291	        # error code should be updated only when a non-empty command is run
292	        if (($null -eq $lastHistory) -or ($script:LastHistoryId -eq $lastHistory.Id)) {
293	            $script:ExecutionTime = 0
294	            $script:NoExitCode = $true
295	            return
296	        }
297	
298	        $script:NoExitCode = $false
299	        $script:LastHistoryId = $lastHistory.Id
300	        $script:ExecutionTime = ($lastHistory.EndExecutionTime - $lastHistory.StartExecutionTime).TotalMilliseconds
301	        if ($script:OriginalLastExecutionStatus) {
302	            $script:ErrorCode = 0
303	            return
304	        }
305	
306	        $invocationInfo = try {
307	            # retrieve info of the most recent error
308	            $global:Error | Where-Object { $_.GetType().Name -eq 'ErrorRecord' } | Select-Object -First 1 -ExpandProperty InvocationInfo
309	        }
310	        catch {
311	            $null
312	        }
313	
314	        # Check if the error occurred in the current command scope
315	        if ($null -ne $invocationInfo -and
316	            $invocationInfo.HistoryId -eq $lastHistory.Id) {
317	            $script:ErrorCode = 1
318	            return
319	        }
320	
321	        if ($script:OriginalLastExitCode -is [int] -and $script:OriginalLastExitCode -ne 0) {
322	            # native app exit code
323	            $script:ErrorCode = $script:OriginalLastExitCode
324	            return
325	        }
326	    }

329	        param(
330	            [string]$Type,
331	            [string[]]$Arguments
332	        )
333	        $nonFSWD = Get-NonFSWD
334	        $stackCount = Get-PoshStackCount
335	        $terminalWidth = Get-TerminalWidth
336	        Invoke-Utf8Posh @(
337	            "print", $Type
338	            "--save-cache"
339	            "--shell=$script:ShellName"
340	            "--shell-version=$script:PSVersion"
341	            "--status=$script:ErrorCode"
342	            "--no-status=$script:NoExitCode"
343	            "--execution-time=$script:ExecutionTime"
344	            "--pswd=$nonFSWD"
345	            "--stack-count=$stackCount"
346	            "--terminal-width=$terminalWidth"
347	            "--job-count=$script:JobCount"
348	            if ($Arguments) {
349	                $Arguments
350	            }
351	        )
352	    }

354	    function Register-PoshStreamingOnIdle {
355	        if ($null -ne $script:StreamingOnIdleJob) {
356	            return
357	        }
358	
359	        # PSReadLine anchors the prompt position when ReadLine starts, and PowerShell.OnIdle
360	        # can only fire while ReadLine is waiting for input, i.e. after that anchor exists.
361	        # That makes OnIdle the earliest safe point to allow redraws (State = 'RUNNING') and
362	        # to flush updates that arrived before the anchor existed. Calling InvokePrompt()
363	        # any earlier redraws at the previous prompt's coordinates.
364	        #
365	        # OnIdle is also the ONLY async consumer of streamed records. It is an
366	        # engine-generated event on the pipeline thread: consuming records here
367	        # instead of in a DataAdded subscription means no PSEvent is ever raised
368	        # from a background thread. Cross-thread event delivery can re-enter the
369	        # engine's pulse pipeline and crash the host with
370	        # InvalidPipelineStateException ("Cannot invoke pipeline because it has
371	        # already been invoked") under rapid prompt cycles.
372	        $script:StreamingOnIdleJob = Register-EngineEvent -SourceIdentifier PowerShell.OnIdle -Action {
373	            $s = $global:_ompStreamingState
374	
375	            # No streaming prompt cycle has ever been started (neither serve nor legacy).
376	            if (-not $s.CycleStarted) {
377	                return
378	            }
379	
380	            if ($s.State -eq 'NEW') {
381	                $s.State = 'RUNNING'
382	            }
383	
384	            # Drain records the reader appended while idle: async segment
385	            # updates and the transient refresh. This runs on the same thread
386	            # as the synchronous waiter, so sharing the cursor is race-free.
387	            & $s.Drain $s
388	
389	            if (-not $s.Dirty) {
390	                return
391	            }
392	
393	            $s.Dirty = $false
394	
395	            $previousOutputEncoding = [Console]::OutputEncoding
396	
397	            try {
398	                [Console]::OutputEncoding = [Text.Encoding]::UTF8
399	                [Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::InvokePrompt()
400	            }
401	            catch {}
402	            finally {
403	                [Console]::OutputEncoding = $previousOutputEncoding
404	            }
405	        }
406	    }

408	    function Stop-StreamingProcess {
409	        if (-not $global:_ompStreaming) {
410	            return
411	        }
412	
413	        # Kill the streaming process
414	        if ($null -ne $script:Streaming.Process -and -not $script:Streaming.Process.HasExited) {
415	            try {
416	                $script:Streaming.Process.Kill()
417	            }
418	            catch {
419	            }
420	        }
421	
422	        $script:Streaming.Process = $null
423	        $script:Streaming.State = 'NEW'
424	        $script:Streaming.Dirty = $false
425	    }
426	
427	    function Stop-ActiveRenderCycle {
428	        # Serve mode: the daemon persists across cycles, only the in-flight
429	        # render needs to be interrupted - write abort instead of killing
430	        # anything. Guard on the process still being alive.
431	        if ($null -ne $script:Streaming.ServeProcess -and -not $script:Streaming.ServeProcess.HasExited) {
432	            try {
433	                $script:Streaming.StdIn.WriteLine('{"command":"abort"}')
434	                $script:Streaming.StdIn.Flush()
435	            }
436	            catch {
437	            }
438	
439	            $script:Streaming.State = 'NEW'
440	            $script:Streaming.Dirty = $false
441	            return
442	        }
443	
444	        # Legacy per-prompt process: nothing to abort, kill it outright.
445	        Stop-StreamingProcess
446	    }
447	
448	    # Chunked reader for NUL-delimited prompt records, shared by the serve
449	    # daemon (which passes a wake signal) and the legacy per-prompt stream
450	    # (which passes $null). Runs in its own runspace for the lifetime of the
451	    # stream it reads.
452	    #
453	    # Reads in 4KB chunks and splits on NUL via [Array]::IndexOf instead of
454	    # one $stream.ReadByte() call per byte: a gradient-heavy prompt record
455	    # can run 1-2KB, and a PowerShell method call per byte to drain it was
456	    # measured adding tens of ms per cycle - enough to push a spammed Enter
457	    # past the ~33ms key-repeat interval and turn "stops instantly on
458	    # release" into "keeps draining for seconds".
459	    $script:StreamingReaderScript = {
460	        param($stream, $signal)
461	
462	        $bufferSize = 4096
463	        $buffer = [byte[]]::new($bufferSize)
464	        $pending = [System.Collections.Generic.List[byte]]::new()
465	
466	        $appendSegment = {
467	            param($segStart, $segEnd)
468	
469	            $segLen = $segEnd - $segStart
470	            if ($segLen -le 0) {
471	                return
472	            }
473	
474	            $segment = [byte[]]::new($segLen)
475	            [Array]::Copy($buffer, $segStart, $segment, 0, $segLen)
476	            $pending.AddRange($segment)
477	        }
478	
479	        while ($true) {
480	            $read = $stream.Read($buffer, 0, $bufferSize)
481	
482	            if ($read -le 0) {
483	                if ($pending.Count -gt 0) {
484	                    Write-Output ([Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetString($pending.ToArray()))
485	                }
486	
487	                # Wake the waiter - also on EOF, so a dying daemon triggers
488	                # the fallback path immediately instead of after the timeout.
489	                if ($signal) {
490	                    $signal.Set()
491	                }
492	
493	                return
494	            }
495	
496	            $offset = 0
497	            while ($offset -lt $read) {
498	                $nul = [Array]::IndexOf($buffer, [byte]0, $offset, $read - $offset)
499	
500	                if ($nul -lt 0) {
501	                    & $appendSegment $offset $read
502	                    $offset = $read
503	                    continue
504	                }
505	
506	                & $appendSegment $offset $nul
507	
508	                if ($pending.Count -gt 0) {
509	                    Write-Output ([Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetString($pending.ToArray()))
510	                    $pending.Clear()
511	                }
512	
513	                if ($signal) {
514	                    $signal.Set()
515	                }
516	
517	                $offset = $nul + 1
518	            }
519	        }
520	    }
521	
522	    function Start-PoshServe {
523	        $Process = New-Object System.Diagnostics.Process
524	        $StartInfo = $Process.StartInfo
525	        $StartInfo.FileName = $global:_ompExecutable
526	
527	        # ArgumentList is supported in PowerShell 6.1+; $script:ServeSupported already
528	        # requires major version 6, but guard defensively like Invoke-Utf8Posh does.
529	        if ($StartInfo.ArgumentList.Add) {
530	            $StartInfo.ArgumentList.Add("serve")
531	            $StartInfo.ArgumentList.Add("--shell=$script:ShellName")
532	        }
533	        else {
534	            $StartInfo.Arguments = "serve --shell=$script:ShellName"
535	        }
536	
537	        # IMPORTANT: BOM-less UTF-8 for stdin. [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8
538	        # emits a BOM preamble on the writer's first write, which would
539	        # corrupt the first JSON request line and make the daemon silently
540	        # drop the first render of every fresh process.
541	        $StartInfo.StandardInputEncoding = [System.Text.UTF8Encoding]::new($false)
542	        $StartInfo.StandardOutputEncoding = [System.Text.UTF8Encoding]::new($false)
543	        $StartInfo.RedirectStandardInput = $true
544	        $StartInfo.RedirectStandardOutput = $true
545	        # stdout carries ONLY protocol records; redirect stderr too so a Go
546	        # panic in the daemon can never spew into the user's terminal - it's
547	        # simply discarded (not read) since we never attach a consumer to it.
548	        $StartInfo.RedirectStandardError = $true
549	        $StartInfo.UseShellExecute = $false
550	        $StartInfo.CreateNoWindow = $true
551	        if ($PWD.Provider.Name -eq 'FileSystem') {
552	            $StartInfo.WorkingDirectory = $PWD.ProviderPath
553	        }
554	
555	        try {
556	            [void]$Process.Start()
557	        }
558	        catch {
559	            return $false
560	        }
561	
562	        # Drain stderr fire-and-forget: an undrained redirected pipe can fill
563	        # up and block the daemon mid-write (e.g. an unrecovered panic's stack
564	        # trace). The content is deliberately discarded.
565	        $null = $Process.StandardError.ReadToEndAsync()
566	
567	        # Read the persistent stdout stream asynchronously for the lifetime of the session.
568	        $output = New-Object 'System.Management.Automation.PSDataCollection[PSObject]'
569	        $inputData = New-Object 'System.Management.Automation.PSDataCollection[PSObject]'
570	        $inputData.Complete()
571	        $signal = [System.Threading.ManualResetEventSlim]::new($false)
572	        $ps = [powershell]::Create().AddScript($script:StreamingReaderScript).AddArgument($Process.StandardOutput.BaseStream).AddArgument($signal)
573	
574	        # There is deliberately NO DataAdded subscription on the collection:
575	        # the reader appends from a background thread, and a PSEvent raised
576	        # from a non-engine thread can re-enter the engine's pulse pipeline
577	        # and crash the host (InvalidPipelineStateException) under rapid
578	        # prompt cycles. Records are consumed exclusively on the engine
579	        # thread: synchronously by Get-PoshStreamingPrompt's waiter, and
580	        # asynchronously by the OnIdle action's drain.
581	        $ps.BeginInvoke($inputData, $output) | Out-Null
582	
583	        $script:Streaming.ServeProcess = $Process
584	        $script:Streaming.StdIn = $Process.StandardInput
585	        # Fresh daemon, fresh collection: nothing consumed yet. The previous
586	        # daemon's signal (if any) is intentionally not disposed - its reader
587	        # may still hold a reference; the GC reclaims it.
588	        $script:Streaming.Output = $output
589	        $script:Streaming.RecordIndex = 0
590	        $script:Streaming.Signal = $signal
591	
592	        return $true
593	    }
594	
595	    function ConvertTo-PoshServeJsonString($Value) {
596	        if ($null -eq $Value) {
597	            return '""'
598	        }
599	
600	        # Minimal, fast escaping for the flat string values we send: backslash
601	        # and double-quote first (order matters), then control characters.
602	        $escaped = $Value.Replace('\', '\\').Replace('"', '\"')
603	        $escaped = $escaped -replace "`r", '\r' -replace "`n", '\n' -replace "`t", '\t'
604	        return '"' + $escaped + '"'
605	    }
606	
607	    function Get-PoshFSWD {
608	        # Serve needs an actual filesystem path for the daemon to os.Chdir into;
609	        # a non-filesystem provider (e.g. a registry drive) has no such path -
610	        # let the daemon keep its previous/last-good working directory.
611	        if ($PWD.Provider.Name -eq 'FileSystem') {
612	            return $PWD.ProviderPath
613	        }
614	        return ''
615	    }

617	    function Get-PoshServeEnvOverlay {
618	        # v1 env overlay: PATH, every POSH_* variable, VIRTUAL_ENV and
619	        # CONDA_PROMPT_MODIFIER. Deliberately not derived from config
620	        # templates yet (see implementation plan TODO).
621	        $overlay = [ordered]@{}
622	        $overlay['PATH'] = $env:PATH
623	
624	        Get-ChildItem env:POSH_* -ErrorAction Ignore | ForEach-Object {
625	            $overlay[$_.Name] = $_.Value
626	        }
627	
628	        if (Test-Path env:VIRTUAL_ENV) {
629	            $overlay['VIRTUAL_ENV'] = $env:VIRTUAL_ENV
630	        }
631	
632	        if (Test-Path env:CONDA_PROMPT_MODIFIER) {
633	            $overlay['CONDA_PROMPT_MODIFIER'] = $env:CONDA_PROMPT_MODIFIER
634	        }
635	
636	        return $overlay
637	    }

639	    function Suspend-PoshServeOnFailure {
640	        $script:Streaming.FailureCount++
641	        if ($script:Streaming.FailureCount -ge 3) {
642	            # Degrade to the per-prompt stream path for the rest of the
643	            # session - a repeatedly failing daemon must not add a restart
644	            # plus a response timeout to every single prompt.
645	            $script:ServeSupported = $false
646	        }
647	    }
648	
649	    function Get-PoshStreamingPrompt {
650	        if (-not $script:ServeSupported) {
651	            return Get-PoshStreamingPromptLegacy
652	        }
653	
654	        Register-PoshStreamingOnIdle
655	
656	        # The reader's record collection only grows - both consumers key off
657	        # add-time indices, so in-place trimming would corrupt their cursors.
658	        # Recycle the daemon once the collection gets large: one slower prompt
659	        # every few thousand beats unbounded growth in long-lived sessions.
660	        if ($null -ne $script:Streaming.Output -and $script:Streaming.Output.Count -ge 4096) {
661	            try {
662	                $script:Streaming.StdIn.WriteLine('{"command":"quit"}')
663	                $script:Streaming.StdIn.Flush()
664	            }
665	            catch {
666	            }
667	            $script:Streaming.ServeProcess = $null
668	        }
669	
670	        if ($null -eq $script:Streaming.ServeProcess -or $script:Streaming.ServeProcess.HasExited) {
671	            if (-not (Start-PoshServe)) {
672	                # Could not start the daemon at all - fall back for this cycle.
673	                Suspend-PoshServeOnFailure
674	                return Get-PoshStreamingPromptLegacy
675	            }
676	        }
677	
678	        $script:Streaming.CycleId++
679	        $script:Streaming.Transient = ''
680	        $script:Streaming.CycleStarted = $true
681	
682	        $envOverlay = Get-PoshServeEnvOverlay
683	        $envJson = ($envOverlay.Keys | ForEach-Object {
684	                '"' + $_ + '":' + (ConvertTo-PoshServeJsonString $envOverlay[$_])
685	            }) -join ','
686	
687	        $json = '{' +
688	        '"command":"render"' +
689	        ',"id":' + $script:Streaming.CycleId +
690	        ',"shell":' + (ConvertTo-PoshServeJsonString $script:ShellName) +
691	        ',"shell-version":' + (ConvertTo-PoshServeJsonString $script:PSVersion) +
692	        ',"status":' + [int]$script:ErrorCode +
693	        ',"no-status":' + $(if ($script:NoExitCode) { 'true' } else { 'false' }) +
694	        ',"execution-time":' + $script:ExecutionTime +
695	        ',"pwd":' + (ConvertTo-PoshServeJsonString (Get-PoshFSWD)) +
696	        ',"pswd":' + (ConvertTo-PoshServeJsonString (Get-NonFSWD)) +
697	        ',"stack-count":' + (Get-PoshStackCount) +
698	        ',"terminal-width":' + (Get-TerminalWidth) +
699	        ',"job-count":' + $script:JobCount +
700	        ',"cleared":false' +
701	        ',"env":{' + $envJson + '}' +
702	        '}'
703	
704	        try {
705	            $script:Streaming.StdIn.WriteLine($json)
706	            $script:Streaming.StdIn.Flush()
707	        }
708	        catch {
709	            # The daemon died between the health check above and this write - restart once.
710	            Suspend-PoshServeOnFailure
711	            # Mirror the timeout path: kill before dropping the reference, so a
712	            # process with a broken stdin but a live body can never be leaked.
713	            try {
714	                $script:Streaming.ServeProcess.Kill()
715	            }
716	            catch {
717	            }
718	            $script:Streaming.ServeProcess = $null
719	            if (-not (Start-PoshServe)) {
720	                return Get-PoshStreamingPromptLegacy
721	            }
722	
723	            try {
724	                $script:Streaming.StdIn.WriteLine($json)
725	                $script:Streaming.StdIn.Flush()
726	            }
727	            catch {
728	                # The legacy path repoints $script:Streaming.Output at its own
729	                # collection - a live daemon must not linger with an orphaned one.
730	                try {
731	                    $script:Streaming.ServeProcess.Kill()
732	                }
733	                catch {
734	                }
735	                $script:Streaming.ServeProcess = $null
736	                return Get-PoshStreamingPromptLegacy
737	            }
738	        }
739	
740	        # Wait for the first primary record of THIS cycle by scanning the
741	        # reader's PSDataCollection with the waiter's PRIVATE cursor. The
742	        # DataAdded action cannot be relied on here (whether it fires during
743	        # this loop depends on the calling context) and must not be raced
744	        # against either - records stay in the collection, so this scan works
745	        # regardless of whether the action also processed them, and the action
746	        # dedupes on unchanged content. Between scans, block on the reader's
747	        # signal (sub-millisecond wake) rather than Start-Sleep (~15.6ms timer
748	        # tick). The bounded Wait keeps the Stopwatch timeout authoritative,
749	        # and re-scanning after every wake makes lost wakeups impossible:
750	        # a record landing after a scan leaves the signal set, so the next
751	        # Wait returns immediately.
752	        $s = $script:Streaming
753	        $output = $s.Output
754	        $signal = $s.Signal
755	        $firstPrompt = $null
756	        $stopwatch = [System.Diagnostics.Stopwatch]::StartNew()
757	
758	        while ($null -eq $firstPrompt -and $stopwatch.ElapsedMilliseconds -lt 2000) {
759	            while ($s.RecordIndex -lt $output.Count) {
760	                $record = $output[$s.RecordIndex]
761	                $s.RecordIndex++
762	
763	                if (-not $record) {
764	                    continue
765	                }
766	
767	                $sep = $record.IndexOf([char]0x1F)
768	                if ($sep -lt 0) {
769	                    continue
770	                }
771	
772	                $id = $record.Substring(0, $sep)
773	                if ($id -ne [string]$s.CycleId) {
774	                    # Stale record from an aborted/previous cycle - discard.
775	                    continue
776	                }
777	
778	                $payload = $record.Substring($sep + 1)
779	
780	                if ($payload -and $payload[0] -eq [char]0x1E) {
781	                    $s.Transient = $payload.Substring(1)
782	                    continue
783	                }
784	
785	                # Keep scanning instead of stopping at the primary: records
786	                # that already arrived in the same burst (typically the
787	                # transient) are consumed for free, without waiting. Later
788	                # records are drained by the OnIdle action.
789	                $s.Prompt = $payload
790	                $firstPrompt = $payload
791	            }
792	
793	            if ($null -eq $firstPrompt) {
794	                [void]$signal.Wait(100)
795	                $signal.Reset()
796	            }
797	        }
798	
799	        if ($null -eq $firstPrompt) {
800	            # Daemon stopped responding - kill it and fall back for this cycle.
801	            Suspend-PoshServeOnFailure
802	            try {
803	                $s.ServeProcess.Kill()
804	            }
805	            catch {
806	            }
807	            $s.ServeProcess = $null
808	            return Get-PoshStreamingPromptLegacy
809	        }
810	
811	        return $firstPrompt
812	    }
813	
814	    function Get-PoshStreamingPromptLegacy {
815	        Register-PoshStreamingOnIdle
816	
817	        # Start streaming process (State stays 'NEW' until the first OnIdle event confirms
818	        # PSReadLine has rendered the initial prompt)
819	        $script:Streaming.Process = New-Object System.Diagnostics.Process
820	        $StartInfo = $script:Streaming.Process.StartInfo
821	        $StartInfo.FileName = $global:_ompExecutable
822	
823	        # The transient prompt for this cycle streams in alongside the primary
824	        # prompt updates, invalidate the previous cycle's version.
825	        $script:Streaming.Transient = ''
826	        $script:Streaming.CycleStarted = $true
827	
828	        # Build arguments array
829	        $Arguments = @(
830	            "stream"
831	            "--save-cache"
832	            "--shell=$script:ShellName"
833	            "--shell-version=$script:PSVersion"
834	            "--status=$script:ErrorCode"
835	            "--no-status=$script:NoExitCode"
836	            "--execution-time=$script:ExecutionTime"
837	            "--pswd=$(Get-NonFSWD)"
838	            "--stack-count=$(Get-PoshStackCount)"
839	            "--terminal-width=$(Get-TerminalWidth)"
840	            "--job-count=$script:JobCount"
841	        )
842	
843	        # Use ArgumentList if available (PowerShell 6.1+), otherwise escape manually

content.thinking:
I'm seeing the initialization order problem — OMP runs first and saves the default prompt function, then completely replaces it with its own version without chaining. When zoxide init runs after, it wraps OMP's prompt function, so the chain should work, but the issue is that OMP isn't preserving the original prompt for proper restoration.

Looking at zoxide's hook mechanism, it saves the current prompt function and wraps it with its own logic that calls the old one first, then runs the zoxide hook. This should theoretically work regardless of order since zoxide just wraps whatever prompt exists at that moment — but the user's testing shows it only breaks with async mode enabled, not with async disabled, even though both use the same initialization order. So the problem isn't about the order of OMP and zoxide initialization itself, it's something specific to how async mode affects the prompt function chaining.

I need to figure out what changes when async is enabled. The Enable-PoshStreaming function gets called somewhere in the initialization, and that's the key difference between the working and broken cases. Let me trace through where that gets triggered in the Go code that generates the init script, and whether it somehow causes the prompt function to be reassigned or modified after zoxide has already wrapped it.

file.content:
package shell

import (
	_ "embed"
	"fmt"
	"strings"
)

//go:embed scripts/omp.ps1
var pwshInit string

func (f Features) Pwsh() Code {
	switch f {
	case Tooltips:
		return "Enable-PoshTooltips"
	case LineError:
		return "Enable-PoshLineError"
	case Transient:
		return "$global:_ompTransientPrompt = $true"
	case Jobs:
		return "$global:_ompJobCount = $true"
	case Azure:
		return "$global:_ompAzure = $true"
	case PoshGit:
		return "$global:_ompPoshGit = $true"
	case FTCSMarks:
		return "$global:_ompFTCSMarks = $true"
	case Upgrade:
		return "& $global:_ompExecutable upgrade --auto"
	case Notice:
		return "& $global:_ompExecutable notice"
	case Streaming:
		return "Enable-PoshStreaming"
	case KeyHandlers:
		return "Enable-KeyHandlers"
	case VIMode:
		return "Enable-PoshVIMode"
	case PromptMark, RPrompt, CursorPositioning, Async:
		fallthrough
	default:
		return ""
	}
}

func quotePwshOrElvishStr(str string) string {
	if str == "" {
		return "''"
	}

	return fmt.Sprintf("'%s'", strings.ReplaceAll(str, "'", "''"))
}

content.thinking:
The async/streaming feature works by appending a line like "Enable-PoshStreaming" to the initialization script after the module import, so all these feature flags get included in the same Invoke-Expression call within the user's profile.

The execution order stays consistent regardless of the async setting: oh-my-posh init runs first to set up the prompt function and enable streaming, then zoxide init wraps around that prompt function entirely. Since zoxide replaces the $Function:prompt with its own wrapper that calls the previous prompt function and appends its hook, this outer wrapper shouldn't be bypassed by anything happening inside oh-my-posh's function body — yet the debug output showed something unexpected happening.

When async is enabled, checking the current prompt function definition reveals no zoxide code at all, which means oh-my-posh's prompt function got reasserted and overwrote zoxide's wrapper after the initial setup. Something is resetting $Function:prompt back to $promptFunction after zoxide wrapped it, and I need to figure out what triggers this when async is enabled — it's not happening in the Register-PoshStreamingOnIdle or Enable-PoshStreaming functions, so there might be a re-entrant issue where PSReadLine's InvokePrompt call during the OnIdle action is somehow causing the prompt function to get re-evaluated or reset.

Actually, I should reconsider the module import order — the synchronous config showed zoxide in the prompt but async didn't, which suggests the issue might be in how the original prompt function is captured. When the New-Module block runs, it backs up $Function:prompt as $script:OriginalPromptFunction at line 41, but if zoxide hasn't run yet in the profile, that backup would be the default built-in prompt, not zoxide's wrapper. Let me check whether "async" is actually a separate config option from the "Streaming" feature and trace through the code to see where Async triggers Enable-PoshStreaming.

Ah, I see it now — the Async feature returns an empty string for PowerShell, meaning it doesn't directly emit Enable-PoshStreaming. That's handled by the separate "Streaming" feature instead. So I need to find where the config's async:true actually maps to in the Features list.

309	// identifies the session cache, and POSH_CONFIG is pinned to the session's

312	// to change the configuration mid-session.

321			return fmt.Sprintf("export POSH_SESSION_ID=\"%s\"; export POSH_CONFIG=%s;", sessionID, QuotePosixStr(config))
