Verify a candidate finding from a code review, empirically, using live PowerShell (`pwsh` is available).

Repo: D:\oh-my-posh. Read src/shell/init.go's `sourceCommandAsync` PWSH case (~line 284-289) for the exact current emitted PowerShell text — it starts with something like `if (-not $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction) { $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction = $Function:prompt }; ...` — get the literal current text from the file, don't trust paraphrase.

## Candidate finding to verify

"Under `Set-StrictMode -Version 2` or `-Version Latest` (common in user PowerShell profiles, set BEFORE the oh-my-posh init line runs), the init line's read of `$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction` — a global variable that has never been assigned anything on a fresh session — throws a 'variable has not been set' error, because StrictMode prohibits referencing uninitialized variables. This would abort the entire `Invoke-Expression` of the init script before the trampoline gets installed, so oh-my-posh never initializes. The OLD pre-fix async placeholder (`function prompt() { & <scriptPath> }`) had no variable reads at all and was immune to this."

## How to verify

Test directly and precisely — StrictMode's exact behavior around unset `$global:` (as opposed to unset local/script-scope) variables is the crux and must not be assumed either way:

```powershell
pwsh -NoProfile -Command '
Set-StrictMode -Version Latest
if (-not $global:_someNeverSetVar_xyz) { Write-Host "condition evaluated fine, value is falsy as expected" } else { Write-Host "unexpected: truthy" }
Write-Host "no error was thrown"
'
```
If that does NOT throw, also test the EXACT literal line from init.go (copy it verbatim from the file) under `Set-StrictMode -Version Latest`, e.g.:
```powershell
pwsh -NoProfile -Command '
Set-StrictMode -Version Latest
<exact literal trampoline install line from init.go, with a placeholder scriptPath like "C:\test.ps1">
Write-Host "install line ran without throwing"
'
```
Also specifically test `Set-StrictMode -Version 1` and `-Version 2` and `-Version Latest` separately if behavior might differ by version (Microsoft's docs distinguish what each version checks).

Also check: does `Set-StrictMode` set in the CALLING scope (the user's profile / interactive session) even apply inside `Invoke-Expression`'d code, and does it carry into a `function prompt() { ... }` DEFINITION (as opposed to its later invocation) — StrictMode variable-checks happen at execution time of the read, so check whether merely DEFINING the function (which happens immediately) vs. later CALLING it is where the unset-variable read actually occurs, since that changes whether init itself fails vs. only the first prompt draw failing later.

## Report

State CONFIRMED (reproduced an actual throw from StrictMode on this exact code — quote the exact error and which StrictMode version/line triggered it), PLAUSIBLE (some uncertainty about which exact PowerShell version/StrictMode level triggers it, but the mechanism is real for at least one config), or REFUTED (tested thoroughly, StrictMode does NOT throw on unset `$global:` scoped variable reads — quote your test output proving this, and explain why, e.g. StrictMode's uninitialized-variable check may only apply to local/script scope, not explicitly-scoped global reads). Show your actual command output, not just a conclusion.

input.description: Verify: Remove-Module can resurrect the module
input.prompt:
Verify a candidate finding from a code review, empirically, using live PowerShell (`pwsh` is available).

Repo: D:\oh-my-posh. Build the test binary: `cd D:\oh-my-posh\src && go build -o omp-verify2.exe .` (delete when done). Use a minimal async theme config:
```yaml
version: 4
async: true
blocks:
  - type: prompt
    alignment: left
    segments:
      - type: text
        style: plain
        template: "TESTPROMPT"
```

Read src/shell/init.go's `sourceCommandAsync` PWSH case and src/shell/scripts/omp.ps1's OnRemove block (search for `OnRemove +=`, roughly lines 1300-1390) for exact current text.

## Candidate finding to verify

"When `$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction` was captured as falsy/`$null` (e.g. because the host had no `prompt` function defined at all when the async init line first ran — a genuinely rare edge case, but also reachable if a user does `Remove-Item Function:prompt` before running the init line), then `Remove-Module -Name oh-my-posh-core -Force` can silently RESURRECT the module instead of cleanly removing it. Mechanism claimed: OnRemove's async branch does `$global:_ompPromptFunction = $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction` (setting it to the falsy/null captured value); the trampoline (which is NEVER removed by Remove-Module — it's a plain global function installed once at init, outside the module) remains installed as `prompt`; on the VERY NEXT prompt draw after removal, the trampoline's `if (-not $global:_ompPromptFunction)` condition is now true again (since it was reset to null/falsy), so it re-triggers the FULL deferred import — reinstalling the module the user just explicitly removed, with no error or indication this happened."

## How to verify

Simulate in `pwsh -NoProfile`:
1. BEFORE running the omp init line at all, ensure there is no `prompt` function: `Remove-Item Function:\prompt -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` (or check `Test-Path Function:\prompt` first — on a truly fresh `pwsh -NoProfile` session, is there a built-in default `prompt` function already defined, or is it genuinely absent until PowerShell's own default gets JIT-defined on first use? This matters — check `Get-Command prompt -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` and `$function:prompt` right at session start before doing anything.)
2. Run `$init = & .\omp-verify2.exe init pwsh --config <cfg>; Invoke-Expression ($init -join "`n")`.
3. Check `$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction` — is it null/empty, given step 1?
4. Call `prompt` (first draw, triggers import). Call `prompt` again (steady state, confirm `TESTPROMPT` renders).
5. Run `Remove-Module -Name oh-my-posh-core -Force`.
6. Check `$global:_ompPromptFunction` right after removal — is it null/falsy?
7. Call `prompt` ONE more time. Does it render `TESTPROMPT` again (meaning the module got silently reinstalled/resurrected), or does it correctly show nothing/a plain prompt (meaning removal stuck)?

Report your actual observations at each numbered step, especially whether PowerShell has a genuinely-undefined `prompt` function at fresh session start (this determines whether the "falsy original" scenario is even reachable in a normal session, or only via the explicit `Remove-Item Function:\prompt` workaround).

## Report

State CONFIRMED (reproduced: after Remove-Module, the next prompt call resurrects the module — show your test output), PLAUSIBLE (mechanism traced correctly in the code but you couldn't get `_ompOriginalPromptFunction` to actually be falsy in a realistic scenario, or some other gap — explain precisely), or REFUTED (tested and Remove-Module cleanly sticks — show output, explain what actually happens, e.g. maybe PowerShell always has SOME default prompt function so this is unreachable in practice). Clean up omp-verify2.exe when done.

<task-notification>
<task-id>abe5544db8e8c0615</task-id>
<tool-use-id>toolu_01YWEzAfXSK2XAK8qG6Y4tdf</tool-use-id>
<output-file>C:\Users\jande\AppData\Local\Temp\claude\D--oh-my-posh\15735695-7dd4-4367-9b11-3a8b95dfbbac\tasks\abe5544db8e8c0615.output</output-file>
<status>completed</status>
<summary>Agent "Verify: $? masking on every async draw" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>REFUTED.

## What I tested

The literal trampoline from `D:\oh-my-posh\src\shell\init.go` (`sourceCommandAsync`, PWSH case):

```
function prompt() { if (-not $global:_ompPromptFunction) { $global:_ompAsyncInit = $true; & %s; return }; if ($global:_ompPromptFunction) { & $global:_ompPromptFunction } }
```

and the real delegation shape from `D:\oh-my-posh\src\shell\scripts\omp.ps1` line 1006 (`$global:_ompPromptFunction = $promptFunction`, a module-bound scriptblock whose first real statement stores `$?` — line ~915).

Ran in `pwsh -NoProfile` on this machine, with the delegated function reading `$?` as its first statement after a failed `Get-Item 'Z:\does-not-exist-xyz' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue`, in four shapes: plain scriptblock, module-bound scriptblock (via `New-Module`, matching omp.ps1 exactly), engine-style `$ExecutionContext.SessionState.InvokeCommand.InvokeScript('prompt')`, and a native-command failure (`cmd /c "exit 5"`).

## Actual output

```
=== Test A: module-bound scriptblock through exact trampoline ===
  [module fn] OriginalLastExecutionStatus = False
=== Test B: sanity, after a SUCCESSFUL command ===
  [module fn] OriginalLastExecutionStatus = True
=== Test C: engine-style invocation (InvokeCommand on 'prompt'), after failure ===
  [module fn] OriginalLastExecutionStatus = False
=== Test D: native command failure (exit code), after failure ===
  [module fn] OriginalLastExecutionStatus = False (LASTEXITCODE=5)
```

`$?` correctly propagates as `$false` through both trampoline `if`s into the delegated real prompt function, in every invocation shape. Success correctly reads `$true` (Test B), so the test isn't stuck-at-false.

## Why the claim is wrong (the interesting part)

The mechanism is half-real, which is presumably what made it convincing. I isolated it:

```
=== Test 4: does evaluating an if-condition variable test reset $? ===
  inside condtest: $? on entry = False, $? after if-condition eval = True
```

The `if (-not $global:_ompPromptFunction)` condition DOES reset `$?` to `$true` — but only as observed by subsequent statements **in the trampoline's own scope**. When `& $global:_ompPromptFunction` invokes the delegated scriptblock, PowerShell gives the callee's first statement the `$?` of the last completed *pipeline* (the user's failed command), not the trampoline's intervening expression evaluations. So the value the omp `$promptFunction` stores in `$script:OriginalLastExecutionStatus` matches the user's real command status.

Confidence in REFUTED is high: exact trampoline text, exact module-bound delegation, engine-style invocation, both failure kinds, plus a positive control — all consistent.</result>

<task-id>a0c7366ff70fbf737</task-id>
<tool-use-id>REDACTED</tool-use-id>
<output-file>C:\Users\jande\AppData\Local\Temp\claude\D--oh-my-posh\15735695-7dd4-4367-9b11-3a8b95dfbbac\tasks\a0c7366ff70fbf737.output</output-file>
<status>completed</status>
<summary>Agent "Verify: StrictMode breaks trampoline init" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>## CONFIRMED

The finding is real and reproduced empirically at every StrictMode level, including 1.0.

### Exact code under test (verbatim from `D:\oh-my-posh\src\shell\init.go`, `sourceCommandAsync`, PWSH case, lines 285-289)

```
if (-not $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction) { $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction = $Function:prompt }; $global:_ompPromptFunction = $null; function prompt() { if (-not $global:_ompPromptFunction) { $global:_ompAsyncInit = $true; & '<scriptPath>'; return }; if ($global:_ompPromptFunction) { & $global:_ompPromptFunction } }
```

### Test 1 — unset `$global:` read under each StrictMode version (pwsh -NoProfile)

```
--- StrictMode 1.0 ---
THREW on global: read -> The variable '$global:_someNeverSetVar_xyz' cannot be retrieved because it has not been set.
--- StrictMode 2.0 ---
THREW on global: read -> The variable '$global:_someNeverSetVar_xyz' cannot be retrieved because it has not been set.
--- StrictMode 3.0 ---
THREW ...
--- StrictMode Latest ---
THREW ...
```

The explicit `global:` scope qualifier does NOT exempt the read — StrictMode's uninitialized-variable check applies to explicitly-scoped reads too, and even version 1.0 triggers it (1.0 only exempts uninitialized variables inside strings).

### Test 2 — the exact literal init line, `Set-StrictMode -Version Latest` in caller, then `Invoke-Expression` (the real init path)

```
INSTALL THREW -> The variable '$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction' cannot be retrieved because it has not been set.
prompt function exists: "PS $($executionContext.SessionState.Path.Curren...   <- still the DEFAULT pwsh prompt
ompOriginalPromptFunction set? False
```

Repeated with `-Version 1.0` and `-Version 2.0`: identical throw both times.

### Key mechanics confirmed

1. **StrictMode set in the calling scope DOES apply inside `Invoke-Expression`'d code** — the IEX'd init line inherits the session's strict mode.
2. **The failure is at init time, not first-prompt time.** The throw happens in the very first statement (`if (-not $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction)`), which executes immediately. It is a terminating error, so the rest of the IEX'd text — including the `function prompt() { ... }` definition — never executes. Test 2 proves it: `prompt` remained the built-in default and `_ompOriginalPromptFunction` was never set. oh-my-posh never installs its trampoline at all.
3. The function *body*'s own reads of `$global:_ompPromptFunction` would be a second, later failure point (at first prompt draw), but it's moot — init aborts before the function is defined.
4. The old placeholder `function prompt() { & <scriptPath> }` contains no variable reads, so it was immune, matching the finding's claim.

Trivial repro one-liner: `pwsh -NoProfile -Command "Set-StrictMode -Version 1; if (-not \$global:_x) { 'ok' }"` → throws.

Fix direction (not applied): use a read that StrictMode permits, e.g. `if (-not (Test-Path Variable:global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction))` or `Get-Variable -Name _ompOriginalPromptFunction -Scope Global -ErrorAction Ignore`, for every first-read of the three globals (`_ompOriginalPromptFunction`, `_ompPromptFunction`, and `_ompAsyncInit`'s later consumers in the generated script would need the same audit).

Relevant file: `D:\oh-my-posh\src\shell\init.go` (emitting code, lines 284-289); test covering the emitted text: `D:\oh-my-posh\src\shell\pwsh_test.go:34`.</result>

The finding is real and reproduced empirically at every StrictMode level, including 1.0.

### Exact code under test (verbatim from `D:\oh-my-posh\src\shell\init.go`, `sourceCommandAsync`, PWSH case, lines 285-289)

```
if (-not $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction) { $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction = $Function:prompt }; $global:_ompPromptFunction = $null; function prompt() { if (-not $global:_ompPromptFunction) { $global:_ompAsyncInit = $true; & '<scriptPath>'; return }; if ($global:_ompPromptFunction) { & $global:_ompPromptFunction } }
```

### Test 1 — unset `$global:` read under each StrictMode version (pwsh -NoProfile)

```
--- StrictMode 1.0 ---
THREW on global: read -> The variable '$global:_someNeverSetVar_xyz' cannot be retrieved because it has not been set.
--- StrictMode 2.0 ---
THREW on global: read -> The variable '$global:_someNeverSetVar_xyz' cannot be retrieved because it has not been set.
--- StrictMode 3.0 ---
THREW ...
--- StrictMode Latest ---
THREW ...
```

The explicit `global:` scope qualifier does NOT exempt the read — StrictMode's uninitialized-variable check applies to explicitly-scoped reads too, and even version 1.0 triggers it (1.0 only exempts uninitialized variables inside strings).

### Test 2 — the exact literal init line, `Set-StrictMode -Version Latest` in caller, then `Invoke-Expression` (the real init path)

```
INSTALL THREW -> The variable '$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction' cannot be retrieved because it has not been set.
prompt function exists: "PS $($executionContext.SessionState.Path.Curren...   <- still the DEFAULT pwsh prompt
ompOriginalPromptFunction set? False
```

Repeated with `-Version 1.0` and `-Version 2.0`: identical throw both times.

### Key mechanics confirmed

1. **StrictMode set in the calling scope DOES apply inside `Invoke-Expression`'d code** — the IEX'd init line inherits the session's strict mode.
2. **The failure is at init time, not first-prompt time.** The throw happens in the very first statement (`if (-not $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction)`), which executes immediately. It is a terminating error, so the rest of the IEX'd text — including the `function prompt() { ... }` definition — never executes. Test 2 proves it: `prompt` remained the built-in default and `_ompOriginalPromptFunction` was never set. oh-my-posh never installs its trampoline at all.
3. The function *body*'s own reads of `$global:_ompPromptFunction` would be a second, later failure point (at first prompt draw), but it's moot — init aborts before the function is defined.
4. The old placeholder `function prompt() { & <scriptPath> }` contains no variable reads, so it was immune, matching the finding's claim.

Trivial repro one-liner: `pwsh -NoProfile -Command "Set-StrictMode -Version 1; if (-not \$global:_x) { 'ok' }"` → throws.

Fix direction (not applied): use a read that StrictMode permits, e.g. `if (-not (Test-Path Variable:global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction))` or `Get-Variable -Name _ompOriginalPromptFunction -Scope Global -ErrorAction Ignore`, for every first-read of the three globals (`_ompOriginalPromptFunction`, `_ompPromptFunction`, and `_ompAsyncInit`'s later consumers in the generated script would need the same audit).

Relevant file: `D:\oh-my-posh\src\shell\init.go` (emitting code, lines 284-289); test covering the emitted text: `D:\oh-my-posh\src\shell\pwsh_test.go:34`.</result>

<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>## CONFIRMED

The resurrection bug is real and reproduced end-to-end in live `pwsh -NoProfile`, but only reachable when `prompt` was explicitly removed before init — a fresh session always has a default `prompt` function, so the normal path is safe.

### Observations per step

**Step 1 — fresh session state.** A fresh `pwsh -NoProfile` session DOES have `prompt` already defined as a real function at startup (`Test-Path Function:\prompt -> True`, `Get-Command prompt -> Function` with the standard `"PS $($executionContext...)>"` body). It is not JIT-defined on first draw. So `$Function:prompt` is a non-null, truthy ScriptBlock in any normal session, and the falsy-original scenario is **only reachable via `Remove-Item Function:\prompt` before the init line** (which the test then did: `Test-Path -> False`).

**Steps 2-3.** After the init line, `$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction` was `null? True; falsy? True` — the `if (-not $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction) { ... = $Function:prompt }` guard in init.go:285 captured `$null`.

**Step 4.** First `prompt` call triggered the deferred import (module loaded, empty first draw), second call rendered `TESTPROMPT`. Normal async behavior.

**Steps 5-6.** `Remove-Module -Name oh-my-posh-core -Force` succeeded (module gone). But OnRemove's async branch (omp.ps1:1357, `$global:_ompPromptFunction = $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction`) set `_ompPromptFunction` back to `$null` — `null? True; falsy? True`.

**Step 7 — resurrection.** The very next `prompt` call hit the trampoline's `if (-not $global:_ompPromptFunction)` branch and silently re-ran the full init: `module resurrected? True`, and the call after that rendered `TESTPROMPT` again. No error, no indication.

### Control run (default prompt intact)

Same flow without removing `prompt` first: original capture truthy; after `Remove-Module` the post-removal draw was the restored default `PS D:\oh-my-posh> `, `module resurrected? False`, and it stayed unloaded. Removal sticks cleanly in the normal case.

### Verdict detail

The claimed mechanism is exactly right: the trampoline installed by `sourceCommandAsync` (D:\oh-my-posh\src\shell\init.go:283-289) is a plain global function that survives `Remove-Module`, and it treats a falsy `$global:_ompPromptFunction` as "not yet initialized" — indistinguishable from "user restored a null original". Severity caveat: since PowerShell always defines a default `prompt` at session start, the trigger requires a user who deleted `prompt` (or a profile that does) before running the init line, so it's an edge case, not a mainline regression. A fix would need OnRemove to distinguish "restore null original" — e.g. restore `$Function:prompt` to the original (or remove the trampoline entirely) instead of relying on the sentinel variable.

Cleanup done: `omp-verify2.exe` deleted, `git status` clean. Test scripts live only in the session scratchpad.</result>

<task-notification>
<task-id>a2bf0828d535ba2f4</task-id>
<tool-use-id>REDACTED</tool-use-id>
<output-file>C:\Users\jande\AppData\Local\Temp\claude\D--oh-my-posh\15735695-7dd4-4367-9b11-3a8b95dfbbac\tasks\a2bf0828d535ba2f4.output</output-file>
<status>completed</status>
<summary>Agent "Verify: sync-to-async switch destroys trampoline" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>## CONFIRMED

The finding reproduces exactly as described, on a real build of HEAD in a clean `pwsh -NoProfile` session. After a sync-to-async transition, omp stops rendering entirely: the prompt reverts to the pre-omp prompt and stays there.

### Test output (verbatim)

Harness: pre-omp prompt is `function global:prompt { "PREOMP> " }`; sync config renders `TESTPROMPT`; async config is identical plus `async: true`; isolated cache via `OMP_CACHE_DIR`.

```
STEP 1 prompt output: <<TESTPROMPT>>                 (sync init works)
STEP 2 _ompOriginalPromptFunction captured:
  "# store the original last command execution status / if ($global:NVS_ORIGINAL_LASTEXECUTIONSTATUS ..."
                                                     (that's omp's OWN sync prompt body, not PREOMP)
STEP 3 first draw output: <<>>                       (trampoline fires deferred import)
STEP 3 function:prompt definition after first draw:
  "PREOMP> "                                         (trampoline is GONE, replaced by pre-omp prompt)
STEP 4 second draw output: <<PREOMP> >>
STEP 4 contains TESTPROMPT: False
STEP 4 _ompPromptFunction set: True
STEP 4 direct _ompPromptFunction call output: <<TESTPROMPT>>
STEP 4 is function:prompt the trampoline: False
```

### Every sub-claim verified

- (a) Stale original capture: after the async re-source, `$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction` holds omp's own sync prompt function (its body contains omp internals like `NVS_ORIGINAL_LASTEXECUTIONSTATUS`), not the true pre-omp prompt. Confirmed at STEP 2.
- (b) Trampoline installs correctly at STEP 2 (`$Function:prompt` is the trampoline).
- (c) On first draw the trampoline runs the deferred init script; its preamble (`D:\oh-my-posh\src\shell\scripts\omp.ps1` lines 1-4) does `Remove-Module -Name "oh-my-posh-core" -Force`. The OLD module instance has `$script:AsyncInit = $false`, so its OnRemove handler (omp.ps1 line 1352-1361) takes the sync branch: `$Function:prompt = $script:OriginalPromptFunction`, clobbering the just-installed trampoline with the old module's backup of the pre-omp prompt. Confirmed at STEP 3: `$Function:prompt` is literally `"PREOMP> "` after the first draw.
- (d) The new module loads with `AsyncInit = $true` and only writes `$global:_ompPromptFunction` (omp.ps1 line 1000-1007), never `$Function:prompt`. `_ompPromptFunction` is populated and renders `TESTPROMPT` when called directly, but nothing ever calls it — the second draw outputs `PREOMP> `. Confirmed at STEP 4.

### Notes

- The reverse transition (async → sync) is not affected by this exact mechanism: the async-mode OnRemove branch only touches `$global:_ompPromptFunction`, and the sync init path unconditionally sets `$Function:prompt` (line 1009).
- A natural fix location: the OnRemove sync branch could skip restoring `$Function:prompt` when the current `$Function:prompt` is no longer this module's own prompt function (i.e., a trampoline or something else already replaced it), or the async trampoline's first-draw path could reinstall itself after the deferred import.
- `omp-verify.exe` deleted; no lingering daemons (sync mode here used the streaming/serve path only within the killed session; process list showed none).</result>

content.thinking:
I've verified all four findings: the $? masking claim doesn't hold up, but the sync-to-async trampoline issue, StrictMode initialization problem, and Remove-Module resurrection bug are all confirmed—though the last one is narrow and requires the prompt to be deleted first. Now I'm ranking the top eight findings by severity, prioritizing correctness bugs over cleanup, altitude, reuse, and efficiency issues.

StrictMode breaking async init is the highest priority—any user with Set-StrictMode in their profile before the init line gets no oh-my-posh customization, and while the error shows in the terminal, the prompt stays broken. The sync-to-async mid-session switch destroying the trampoline ranks second since it's reachable through a common workflow of editing the config and re-sourcing without restarting. The Remove-Module resurrection bug is lower priority as an edge case. There's also a redundant second `if` guard that's just cleanup, and I'm noting some reuse opportunities in the async init function.

The OnRemove handler conflates "restored" with "initialized" using the same sentinel variable, which is the root cause underlying both the Remove-Module issue and the null-original problem—I could merge these as one systemic finding about how the trampoline's initialization check and async restore share the same sentinel. There's also a minor efficiency angle about dropping a redundant global read, but that overlaps with the simplification finding so I'll dedupe and keep just one.

With a cap of 8 findings and correctness-first ranking, I'm settling on 5-6 solid findings, starting with StrictMode breaking the entire async init line in the shell initialization code.

Now I'm listing out the confirmed issues: the sync-to-async live switch that destroys the trampoline through the old module's OnRemove branch, the Remove-Module resurrection when the original prompt capture was falsy, a cleanup issue with a redundant guard in the trampoline, and a maintenance risk where the async init global is deliberately excluded from the existing global-enablers block without explanation—which creates a risk that future tidying could reintroduce the original bug.

The test coverage gap is that the PowerShell test only asserts the Go string against itself rather than testing actual behavior, so it wouldn't have caught these bugs. That gives me six findings total, which is within my target range. The fish/bash/zsh findings from the altitude analysis are out of scope since those files aren't touched by this diff, though they're worth mentioning in the prose explanation.

I notice findings 5 and 6 from the altitude angle partially overlap with the reuse angle findings, so I should deduplicate those. I'll write concise descriptions with short summaries under 60 characters and category slugs, then call ReportFindings.

findings.summary: The async trampoline's first statement reads an unset $global: variable, which throws under Set-StrictMode, aborting init before the trampoline is ever installed
findings.short_summary: StrictMode profile breaks async pwsh init entirely
findings.failure_scenario: A user with `Set-StrictMode -Version 1` (or 2/Latest) earlier in their PowerShell profile runs `oh-my-posh init pwsh --config X | iex` with `async: true`. The emitted line's `if (-not $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction) { ... }` reads a global that has never been assigned; StrictMode throws "The variable '$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction' cannot be retrieved because it has not been set" as a terminating error, so the rest of the line — including the `function prompt() {...}` definition — never executes. Verified live: reproduced at StrictMode versions 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, and Latest; the old pre-fix placeholder had no variable reads and was immune.

findings.summary: Switching a live session from sync to async mode re-clobbers the trampoline via the OLD sync module's OnRemove branch, permanently silencing omp
findings.short_summary: Sync-to-async mid-session switch kills the prompt
findings.failure_scenario: User has a sync session running, flips `async: true` in config, re-sources the init line. The trampoline installs and captures `$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction = $Function:prompt` — but that's omp's OWN sync prompt function, not the true original. On the first draw, the deferred script's `Remove-Module -Force` preamble fires the OLD module's OnRemove; that instance's `$script:AsyncInit` is `$false` (it loaded sync), so it takes the sync branch and does `$Function:prompt = $script:OriginalPromptFunction`, overwriting the just-installed trampoline with the old module's own pre-omp backup. The new async module then only ever writes `$global:_ompPromptFunction`, which nothing calls anymore. Verified live end-to-end: second draw after the switch renders the pre-omp prompt, not the theme, and stays that way.

findings.summary: OnRemove's async restore reuses the same falsy sentinel the trampoline treats as "not yet initialized", so Remove-Module can silently resurrect the module it just removed
findings.short_summary: Remove-Module can silently reinstall itself
findings.failure_scenario: If `$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction` was captured as falsy (reachable when `Remove-Item Function:\prompt` ran before the init line — not reachable in a normal session, since pwsh always defines a default `prompt`), `Remove-Module -Name oh-my-posh-core -Force` sets `$global:_ompPromptFunction` back to that falsy value via OnRemove. The trampoline (never removed by Remove-Module — it's a plain global function outside the module) treats that falsy value as "not yet initialized" on the next draw and silently re-runs the full deferred import, reinstalling the just-removed module with no error. Verified live: reproduced end-to-end; the normal case (default prompt present) removes cleanly, confirmed by a control run.

findings.summary: The trampoline's second `if ($global:_ompPromptFunction)` guard is unreachable-as-false within the same invocation and can never fire differently from the first check
findings.short_summary: Second trampoline guard is provably dead code
findings.failure_scenario: The first block always ends in `return`, so the second `if` is only ever reached when the first condition was false — meaning `$global:_ompPromptFunction` is already known truthy, unchanged in between (single-threaded, no intervening statement touches it). A reader will assume it guards a real third state (import ran but didn't set the pointer) that cannot occur in this control flow; `if (-not $X) { ...; return }; & $X` is behaviorally identical with one fewer conditional.

findings.summary: $global:_ompAsyncInit is the only $global:_omp* flag not declared in the existing global-enablers block, with no comment there warning why it can't be
findings.short_summary: _ompAsyncInit breaks the global-flag declaration pattern
findings.failure_scenario: Every other $global:_omp* flag (_ompJobCount, _ompFTCSMarks, _ompStreaming, etc.) is declared with a $false default at lines 20-27 and flipped by feature snippets. _ompAsyncInit is instead created ad hoc from the Go-emitted trampoline string with no matching declaration here — for a real reason (that block runs after the trampoline sets the flag, so a $false default there would stomp it before line 44 consumes it), but that reason is undocumented at the block itself. A future contributor "tidying" the globals for consistency adds `$global:_ompAsyncInit = $false` to the enablers block, silently forcing the sync branch in async mode and reintroducing the exact prompt-clobbering bug #7714 fixed.

findings.summary: The new test asserts the emitted trampoline string equals a copy of itself, so it cannot catch any of the confirmed behavioral bugs above or future drift between init.go and omp.ps1's variable names
findings.short_summary: New test checks string equality, not behavior
findings.failure_scenario: TestSourceCommandAsyncPwsh pins the exact Go output but never executes it, so it stays green through all three confirmed bugs (StrictMode throw, sync-to-async clobber, Remove-Module resurrection) and would stay green if a future edit renamed `_ompPromptFunction` in omp.ps1 without updating init.go's string, silently reverting every draw to a full re-import.

content.content: 6 findings reported.

findings.summary: The async trampoline's first statement reads an unset $global: variable, which throws under Set-StrictMode, aborting init before the trampoline is ever installed
findings.short_summary: StrictMode profile breaks async pwsh init entirely
findings.failure_scenario: A user with `Set-StrictMode -Version 1` (or 2/Latest) earlier in their PowerShell profile runs `oh-my-posh init pwsh --config X | iex` with `async: true`. The emitted line's `if (-not $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction) { ... }` reads a global that has never been assigned; StrictMode throws "The variable '$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction' cannot be retrieved because it has not been set" as a terminating error, so the rest of the line — including the `function prompt() {...}` definition — never executes. Verified live: reproduced at StrictMode versions 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, and Latest; the old pre-fix placeholder had no variable reads and was immune.

findings.summary: Switching a live session from sync to async mode re-clobbers the trampoline via the OLD sync module's OnRemove branch, permanently silencing omp
findings.short_summary: Sync-to-async mid-session switch kills the prompt
findings.failure_scenario: User has a sync session running, flips `async: true` in config, re-sources the init line. The trampoline installs and captures `$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction = $Function:prompt` — but that's omp's OWN sync prompt function, not the true original. On the first draw, the deferred script's `Remove-Module -Force` preamble fires the OLD module's OnRemove; that instance's `$script:AsyncInit` is `$false` (it loaded sync), so it takes the sync branch and does `$Function:prompt = $script:OriginalPromptFunction`, overwriting the just-installed trampoline with the old module's own pre-omp backup. The new async module then only ever writes `$global:_ompPromptFunction`, which nothing calls anymore. Verified live end-to-end: second draw after the switch renders the pre-omp prompt, not the theme, and stays that way.

findings.summary: OnRemove's async restore reuses the same falsy sentinel the trampoline treats as "not yet initialized", so Remove-Module can silently resurrect the module it just removed
findings.short_summary: Remove-Module can silently reinstall itself
findings.failure_scenario: If `$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction` was captured as falsy (reachable when `Remove-Item Function:\prompt` ran before the init line — not reachable in a normal session, since pwsh always defines a default `prompt`), `Remove-Module -Name oh-my-posh-core -Force` sets `$global:_ompPromptFunction` back to that falsy value via OnRemove. The trampoline (never removed by Remove-Module — it's a plain global function outside the module) treats that falsy value as "not yet initialized" on the next draw and silently re-runs the full deferred import, reinstalling the just-removed module with no error. Verified live: reproduced end-to-end; the normal case (default prompt present) removes cleanly, confirmed by a control run.

findings.summary: The trampoline's second `if ($global:_ompPromptFunction)` guard is unreachable-as-false within the same invocation and can never fire differently from the first check
findings.short_summary: Second trampoline guard is provably dead code
findings.failure_scenario: The first block always ends in `return`, so the second `if` is only ever reached when the first condition was false — meaning `$global:_ompPromptFunction` is already known truthy, unchanged in between (single-threaded, no intervening statement touches it). A reader will assume it guards a real third state (import ran but didn't set the pointer) that cannot occur in this control flow; `if (-not $X) { ...; return }; & $X` is behaviorally identical with one fewer conditional.

findings.summary: $global:_ompAsyncInit is the only $global:_omp* flag not declared in the existing global-enablers block, with no comment there warning why it can't be
findings.short_summary: _ompAsyncInit breaks the global-flag declaration pattern
findings.failure_scenario: Every other $global:_omp* flag (_ompJobCount, _ompFTCSMarks, _ompStreaming, etc.) is declared with a $false default at lines 20-27 and flipped by feature snippets. _ompAsyncInit is instead created ad hoc from the Go-emitted trampoline string with no matching declaration here — for a real reason (that block runs after the trampoline sets the flag, so a $false default there would stomp it before line 44 consumes it), but that reason is undocumented at the block itself. A future contributor "tidying" the globals for consistency adds `$global:_ompAsyncInit = $false` to the enablers block, silently forcing the sync branch in async mode and reintroducing the exact prompt-clobbering bug #7714 fixed.

findings.summary: The new test asserts the emitted trampoline string equals a copy of itself, so it cannot catch any of the confirmed behavioral bugs above or future drift between init.go and omp.ps1's variable names
findings.short_summary: New test checks string equality, not behavior
findings.failure_scenario: TestSourceCommandAsyncPwsh pins the exact Go output but never executes it, so it stays green through all three confirmed bugs (StrictMode throw, sync-to-async clobber, Remove-Module resurrection) and would stay green if a future edit renamed `_ompPromptFunction` in omp.ps1 without updating init.go's string, silently reverting every draw to a full re-import.

content.thinking:
I'm focusing on fixing three confirmed bugs, plus a dead-code cleanup that's low-risk and straightforward. For the StrictMode issue where unset global reads throw, I need to use a safe read pattern instead—either checking if the variable exists first with `Test-Path` or using `Get-Variable` with error suppression to avoid triggering the exception.

The key insight is that `$global:_ompPromptFunction = $null` actually counts as initialization for StrictMode purposes, so reading that variable later won't throw even if it's null. The real problem is only the `_ompOriginalPromptFunction` read at the top, since that variable might never have been assigned before. I need to apply the safe read pattern specifically to that check in the emitted trampoline line.

But there's another related issue: in omp.ps1 itself, the line `$script:AsyncInit = [bool]$global:_ompAsyncInit` reads a global that may never have been set on the sync path, which would also throw under StrictMode. Since StrictMode is dynamically scoped and propagates into scripts invoked via the call operator, this becomes a problem when users run sync init with StrictMode active. This is the same class of bug introduced by the same commit, so it's worth fixing too. I'm thinking through using `Get-Variable` with `-ErrorAction Ignore` and `-ValueOnly` to safely check if these globals exist — this cmdlet approach avoids the StrictMode variable dereference issue entirely since it's a proper cmdlet call rather than direct variable access, making it universally safe across both the async and sync initialization paths. Refining the approach with `Get-Variable`...

I'm applying the same StrictMode-safe pattern to the second problematic read at line 44, where `$global:_ompAsyncInit` gets evaluated without being set first. Using `Get-Variable` with `-ErrorAction Ignore` avoids the direct variable dereference that triggers StrictMode violations, letting the check safely default to false when the variable doesn't exist. $null is still a valid initialized state, so StrictMode won't complain about reading it — the variable exists and has a defined value, even if that value is nothing.

The real concern is whether `[bool]$global:_ompAsyncInit` inside the `New-Module` scriptblock respects the caller's StrictMode setting, since modules have their own scope and typically run with StrictMode disabled unless explicitly inherited.  Rather than trying to verify this empirically, I'll apply the safe-read fix defensively to both locations — it's minimal overhead and eliminates any risk.

Now for the second bug about the sync-to-async switch: the OnRemove handler in the old module's sync branch might skip restoring the original prompt function if the current prompt is no longer the module's own version, which breaks the trampoline mechanism.

Actually, this is the same clobbering problem as the original issue #7714, just happening in the sync removal path instead of the async install path. The original fix addressed the async side but left both the sync OnRemove and sync install paths equally vulnerable to overwriting whatever wrapper or tool has taken over the prompt function.

The solution is to make the sync OnRemove branch smarter: before restoring the original prompt function, I should check whether the current `$Function:prompt` still matches the module's own prompt scriptblock reference. If they're the same object, it's safe to restore; if something else has taken over (another trampoline initialization or a third-party wrapper), I should leave it alone and avoid the clobber.

Updating the OnRemove sync branch...

Now I'm thinking through the comparison logic — when checking if `$Function:prompt` equals this module's `$promptFunction`, PowerShell's `-eq` operator on ScriptBlock objects uses reference equality since ScriptBlock doesn't override the Equals method. Since the variable holds the same ScriptBlock object that was originally bound at install time, this comparison should work correctly to detect whether the current prompt is still this module's own function.

I'm also wondering whether the OnRemove handler closure properly captures the module-scoped `$promptFunction` variable — the handler is registered directly without explicitly calling GetNewClosure, so I need to check how the module scope binding works in the actual omp.ps1 source code. so OnRemove fires synchronously in the same runspace, which means `$promptFunction` and the other script-scoped variables are directly accessible without needing `$global:` or any special closure workaround — the module scope is preserved during the OnRemove action execution.

The fix checks whether the current prompt function binding still matches the one this module installed; if it does, restore the original, but if something else has replaced it in the meantime, leave it alone to avoid clobbering a newer trampoline or third-party tool. = $Function:prompt }` — at this point, `$Function:prompt` still holds the OLD module's promptFunction from the prior sync session. Then the async init clears `$global:_ompPromptFunction` and immediately defines a new `prompt` function, which synchronously overwrites `$Function:prompt` with the NEW trampoline before the async init line finishes executing. So when the trampoline eventually runs on the first draw, it invokes the script that was queued up, and the guard logic correctly identifies that the current prompt is already the trampoline, not the original function.

This fixes the second bug while preserving the normal sync-mode behavior — when a user directly removes the module without anything else modifying prompt in between, the comparison still matches and the restore proceeds as expected.

For the async branch, the `_ompPromptFunction` pointer gets reset to its original value without touching `$Function:prompt` itself, which avoids clobbering anything. The real issue with bug 3 (resurrection on re-import) comes from a separate problem where a falsy original function causes the trampoline to think it was never initialized — that needs its own fix rather than changes to the async removal logic.

The core of bug 3 is that the trampoline uses the same sentinel value (falsy `_ompPromptFunction`) to mean both "intentionally removed" and "never initialized yet," so I need to distinguish between these two states. The approach is to introduce a dedicated flag that explicitly marks when the module was removed, so the trampoline knows not to re-import. The real issue is that I'm conflating two different states with the same null value. Instead of relying on `_ompPromptFunction` being null to mean "not initialized," I should introduce a separate flag like `_ompModuleLoaded` that the module sets to true on first load, letting me distinguish between "never touched" and "explicitly removed." but that would prevent legitimate re-initialization unless we reset `_ompModuleLoaded` back to false when re-running init.go's setup. So the strategy is: OnRemove leaves `_ompModuleLoaded` true to block auto-reimport, while an explicit re-init resets it to false to allow the check to trigger again. Now I need to clarify what each variable tracks — `_ompPromptFunction` holds the actual callable to invoke.

Writing trampoline logic... In the async branch of the OnRemove handler, I'm restoring the original prompt function and marking the module as settled so it won't re-trigger the import logic. Then walking through the bug scenario: if the original prompt was falsy, after removal `_ompPromptFunction` becomes null but `_ompInitialized` stays true, so on the next draw the trampoline skips the import branch since initialization is already marked complete, and the null check prevents any execution.

For a normal removal where the original was a real prompt function, it gets restored correctly and the settled flag prevents re-importing. And when a user manually re-runs the init line, the prelude resets both flags so the trampoline properly re-triggers the import on the next draw. This is a minimal fix that adds just one new global variable without touching the rest of the logic.

I should also apply the same safe-read pattern to `_ompInitialized` to handle StrictMode consistently. And I need to consider whether the cleanup convention in OnRemove should include removing this new global variable along with the existing ones.

Actually, looking at the existing pattern in the file, `_ompPromptFunction` and `_ompOriginalPromptFunction` are intentionally left in place after OnRemove so the trampoline continues working correctly — their persistence is the whole point. I should follow that same approach for `_ompInitialized` and add a clarifying comment explaining why these three variables aren't cleaned up, keeping it minimal and consistent with the existing code style while staying focused on the three confirmed bugs.

Now I'm checking whether the second guard condition `if ($global:_ompPromptFunction)` becomes redundant with my new design — the first branch always returns after initialization, so if we reach the second if, we know `_ompInitialized` was already true, which means `_ompPromptFunction` should be set.

Wait, that's not quite right. The two ifs now check different variables: `_ompInitialized` gates the first branch, while `_ompPromptFunction` gates the second. Since `_ompPromptFunction` can legitimately be null even when `_ompInitialized` is true (in edge cases where the original was falsy), the second check is actually meaningful and required, not dead code. So my bug fix for finding 3 naturally resolves the dead code issue from finding 4 as a side effect — two problems solved at once.

Now I need to apply the safe-read pattern to all first-touch global reads for the StrictMode fix: `_ompOriginalPromptFunction` in init.go and `_ompAsyncInit` in omp.ps1. Looking at omp.ps1 around line 44, there's a bare read of `$global:_ompAsyncInit` that's unsafe on the first sync run under StrictMode — I need to wrap that with a safe-read check. As for `_ompInitialized`, omp.ps1 doesn't actually need to read it directly, so that's not a concern there.

The trampoline function that gets emitted in init.go does read `$global:_ompInitialized` to check if initialization is complete, but by the time the trampoline is invoked, the prelude has already run synchronously and assigned that variable, so that read is safe. So there are two specific unsafe reads I need to address: the prelude check for `_ompOriginalPromptFunction` that happens before assignment, and the read of `_ompAsyncInit` which may never be assigned in sync mode. I'll use the `Get-Variable` cmdlet pattern to safely handle both cases, starting with fixing the prelude logic.

Fixing the unsafe reads... When a command produces no output in a boolean context, PowerShell treats it as falsy, so `-not (empty-result)` evaluates to true — which is exactly what we need to detect when a variable isn't set. I should verify this behavior quickly to be sure rather than rely on assumption.

I'll write the actual code and run smoke tests against all three bug fixes plus regression checks using my existing test harness in PowerShell, which is more reliable than reasoning through the edge cases abstractly. Before committing, I should also check if the codebase already has an idiomatic pattern for safe unset-global reads in StrictMode — let me search for existing Get-Variable or Test-Path Variable: usage.

    # Async mode: state is threaded through $global:_ompAsyncInit (set by the

    $script:AsyncInit = [bool]$global:_ompAsyncInit

if ($script:AsyncInit) {
        # Never touch the global prompt binding after the trampoline installed
        # it - anything wrapping it (e.g. another tool's prompt hook) must
        # keep a valid reference forever. Export-ModuleMember below becomes a
        # silent no-op with no function named "prompt" in this module, which
        # is intentional. $global:_ompInitialized is deliberately separate
        # from $global:_ompPromptFunction: it only ever means "don't
        # re-import", so OnRemove can restore a falsy original prompt without
        # the trampoline mistaking that for "never initialized" and silently
        # reinstalling this module on the next draw.
        $global:_ompPromptFunction = $promptFunction
        $global:_ompInitialized = $true
    }
    else {
        $Function:prompt = $promptFunction
    }

input.new_string:
if ($script:AsyncInit) {
                # Restore from the trampoline's own capture, never from
                # $script:OriginalPromptFunction - in async mode that backup
                # holds the wrapper chain (e.g. another tool's prompt hook),
                # and restoring it here would recurse infinitely. Keep
                # $global:_ompInitialized true even when the captured
                # original is falsy, so the trampoline treats this as
                # "restored", not "never initialized" - otherwise the next
                # draw would silently reinstall the module Remove-Module just
                # removed.
                $global:_ompPromptFunction = $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction
                $global:_ompInitialized = $true
            }
            else {
                # Only restore if this module's own prompt function is still
                # the live global binding. If something replaced it since
                # (e.g. a fresh async trampoline installed while switching
                # this session from sync to async), leave it alone instead of
                # clobbering whatever now owns the prompt.
                if ($Function:prompt -eq $promptFunction) {
                    $Function:prompt = $script:OriginalPromptFunction
                }

                # holds the wrapper chain (e.g. another tool's prompt hook),
                # and restoring it here would recurse infinitely. Keep
                # $global:_ompInitialized true even when the captured
                # original is falsy, so the trampoline treats this as
                # "restored", not "never initialized" - otherwise the next
                # draw would silently reinstall the module Remove-Module just
                # removed.
                $global:_ompPromptFunction = $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction
                $global:_ompInitialized = $true
            }
            else {
                # Only restore if this module's own prompt function is still
                # the live global binding. If something replaced it since
                # (e.g. a fresh async trampoline installed while switching
                # this session from sync to async), leave it alone instead of
                # clobbering whatever now owns the prompt.
                if ($Function:prompt -eq $promptFunction) {
                    $Function:prompt = $script:OriginalPromptFunction
                }

Remove-Variable -Name _ompStreamingState -Scope Global -ErrorAction Ignore

            Remove-Item Function:Get-PoshStackCount -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue

            if ($script:AsyncInit) {
                # Restore from the trampoline's own capture, never from
                # $script:OriginalPromptFunction - in async mode that backup
                # holds the wrapper chain (e.g. another tool's prompt hook),
                # and restoring it here would recurse infinitely. Keep
                # $global:_ompInitialized true even when the captured
                # original is falsy, so the trampoline treats this as
                # "restored", not "never initialized" - otherwise the next
                # draw would silently reinstall the module Remove-Module just
                # removed.
                $global:_ompPromptFunction = $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction
                $global:_ompInitialized = $true
            }
            else {
                # Only restore if this module's own prompt function is still
                # the live global binding. If something replaced it since
                # (e.g. a fresh async trampoline installed while switching
                # this session from sync to async), leave it alone instead of
                # clobbering whatever now owns the prompt.
                if ($Function:prompt -eq $promptFunction) {
                    $Function:prompt = $script:OriginalPromptFunction
                }
            }

            (Get-PSReadLineOption).ContinuationPrompt = $script:OriginalContinuationPrompt
            (Get-PSReadLineOption).PromptText = $script:OriginalPromptText

I need to write a comprehensive PowerShell smoke test script that covers the zoxide-wrapper scenario where the first draw call returns blank but the second renders properly, verifies the wrapper persists, tests re-initialization to confirm it resets and re-wraps correctly, validates that removing the module restores the original prompt cleanly without recursion, ensures StrictMode doesn't cause the init line to throw, and handles the edge case where the prompt function is removed before initialization so that removing the module afterward doesn't resurrect it.

Adding a test for switching between sync and async configurations mid-session — the trampoline mechanism needs to survive the transition and the prompt must continue rendering correctly. I'll create this as a single script in the scratchpad directory, using separate config files for sync and async modes as needed.

input.content:
$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
$exe = 'D:\oh-my-posh\src\omp-test.exe'
$cfgAsync = 'C:\Users\jande\AppData\Local\Temp\claude\D--oh-my-posh\15735695-7dd4-4367-9b11-3a8b95dfbbac\scratchpad\mwe.yaml'
$cfgSync = 'C:\Users\jande\AppData\Local\Temp\claude\D--oh-my-posh\15735695-7dd4-4367-9b11-3a8b95dfbbac\scratchpad\mwe-sync.yaml'

function Test-Result($name, $cond) {
    if ($cond) { Write-Host "PASS: $name" -ForegroundColor Green }
    else { Write-Host "FAIL: $name" -ForegroundColor Red }
}

# ============================================================
# Group 1: original scenario, in a FRESH process (isolated via
# pwsh -NoProfile -Command per group so state doesn't bleed)
# ============================================================
Write-Host "--- Group 1: wrapper survival, blank first draw, re-init, normal removal ---"
& pwsh -NoProfile -Command @"
`$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
`$exe = '$exe'; `$cfg = '$cfgAsync'
`$init = & `$exe init pwsh --config `$cfg
Invoke-Expression (`$init -join "``n")

`$global:__wc = 0
`$__old = `$function:prompt
function prompt { `$global:__wc++; if (`$null -ne `$__old) { & `$__old } }

`$p1 = prompt
Write-Host "draw1 blank: `$([string]::IsNullOrEmpty(`$p1))"
`$p2 = prompt
Write-Host "draw2 has TESTPROMPT: `$(`$p2 -match 'TESTPROMPT')"
Write-Host "wrapper calls after 2 draws: `$global:__wc"

`$origBefore = `$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction
Remove-Module -Name oh-my-posh-core -Force
Write-Host "onremove restored to true original: `$(`$global:_ompPromptFunction -eq `$origBefore)"
`$callsBefore = `$global:__wc
`$p3 = prompt
Write-Host "post-removal no throw, wrapper still ran: `$(`$global:__wc -eq (`$callsBefore + 1))"
Write-Host "post-removal no omp output: `$(`$p3 -notmatch 'TESTPROMPT')"
"@

# ============================================================
# Group 2: StrictMode profile - init line must not throw
# ============================================================
Write-Host ""
Write-Host "--- Group 2: StrictMode compatibility ---"
& pwsh -NoProfile -Command @"
`$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
Set-StrictMode -Version Latest
`$exe = '$exe'; `$cfg = '$cfgAsync'
try {
    `$init = & `$exe init pwsh --config `$cfg
    Invoke-Expression (`$init -join "``n")
    Write-Host "init did not throw under StrictMode: True"
} catch {
    Write-Host "init did not throw under StrictMode: False - `$(`$_.Exception.Message)"
}
`$p1 = prompt
`$p2 = prompt
Write-Host "prompt still renders under StrictMode: `$(`$p2 -match 'TESTPROMPT')"
"@

# ============================================================
# Group 3: Remove-Module resurrection with a falsy original prompt
# ============================================================
Write-Host ""
Write-Host "--- Group 3: Remove-Module must not resurrect when original prompt was falsy ---"
& pwsh -NoProfile -Command @"
`$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
`$exe = '$exe'; `$cfg = '$cfgAsync'
Remove-Item Function:\prompt -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
`$init = & `$exe init pwsh --config `$cfg
Invoke-Expression (`$init -join "``n")
Write-Host "original captured falsy as expected: `$(-not `$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction)"

`$p1 = prompt
`$p2 = prompt
Write-Host "renders before removal: `$(`$p2 -match 'TESTPROMPT')"

Remove-Module -Name oh-my-posh-core -Force
`$p3 = prompt
Write-Host "post-removal output is blank (not resurrected): `$([string]::IsNullOrEmpty(`$p3))"
`$p4 = prompt
Write-Host "second post-removal draw still blank (module did NOT reinstall): `$([string]::IsNullOrEmpty(`$p4))"
"@

# ============================================================
# Group 4: sync-to-async live switch must not destroy the trampoline
# ============================================================
Write-Host ""
Write-Host "--- Group 4: sync-to-async switch mid-session ---"
& pwsh -NoProfile -Command @"
`$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
function global:prompt { "PREOMP> " }
`$exe = '$exe'; `$cfgSync = '$cfgSync'; `$cfgAsync = '$cfgAsync'

`$initSync = & `$exe init pwsh --config `$cfgSync
Invoke-Expression (`$initSync -join "``n")
`$p1 = prompt
Write-Host "sync draw renders: `$(`$p1 -match 'TESTPROMPT')"

`$initAsync = & `$exe init pwsh --config `$cfgAsync
Invoke-Expression (`$initAsync -join "``n")
`$p2 = prompt
Write-Host "first async draw after switch is blank: `$([string]::IsNullOrEmpty(`$p2))"
`$p3 = prompt
Write-Host "second async draw after switch renders TESTPROMPT (trampoline survived): `$(`$p3 -match 'TESTPROMPT')"
"@

findings.summary: The async trampoline's first statement reads an unset $global: variable, which throws under Set-StrictMode, aborting init before the trampoline is ever installed
findings.short_summary: StrictMode profile breaks async pwsh init entirely
findings.failure_scenario: A user with `Set-StrictMode -Version 1` (or 2/Latest) earlier in their PowerShell profile runs `oh-my-posh init pwsh --config X | iex` with `async: true`. The emitted line's `if (-not $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction) { ... }` reads a global that has never been assigned; StrictMode throws before the trampoline is defined.

findings.summary: Switching a live session from sync to async mode re-clobbers the trampoline via the OLD sync module's OnRemove branch, permanently silencing omp
findings.short_summary: Sync-to-async mid-session switch kills the prompt
findings.failure_scenario: User flips `async: true` and re-sources the init line mid-session; the OLD sync module's OnRemove unconditionally restored `$Function:prompt` to its own backup, overwriting the just-installed new trampoline.

findings.summary: OnRemove's async restore reused the same falsy sentinel the trampoline treats as "not yet initialized", so Remove-Module could silently resurrect the module it just removed
findings.short_summary: Remove-Module could silently reinstall itself
findings.failure_scenario: When the captured original prompt was falsy, OnRemove set `$global:_ompPromptFunction` back to that falsy value, and the still-installed trampoline treated it as "never initialized", re-running the deferred import on the next draw.

findings.summary: The trampoline's second `if ($global:_ompPromptFunction)` guard was unreachable-as-false within the same invocation, checking the same variable the first branch had just confirmed truthy
findings.short_summary: Second trampoline guard was provably dead code
findings.failure_scenario: Resolved as a side effect of the bug-3 fix: the gating variable for "should I import" is now `_ompInitialized`, distinct from `_ompPromptFunction`, so the second guard now checks a genuinely independent condition (needed for the falsy-original edge case) instead of re-testing the same variable.

findings.summary: $global:_ompAsyncInit is the only $global:_omp* flag not declared in the existing global-enablers block, with no comment there warning why it can't be
findings.short_summary: _ompAsyncInit breaks the global-flag declaration pattern
findings.failure_scenario: A future contributor "tidying" the globals adds `$global:_ompAsyncInit = $false` to the enablers block for consistency, silently forcing the sync branch in async mode.

findings.summary: The trampoline test asserts the emitted string equals a copy of itself, so it cannot catch behavioral bugs or future drift between init.go and omp.ps1's variable names
findings.short_summary: New test checks string equality, not behavior
findings.failure_scenario: TestSourceCommandAsyncPwsh pins the exact Go output but never executes it against a real PowerShell session, so it would stay green through a future variable-name drift between init.go and omp.ps1 that silently breaks the trampoline at runtime.

content.text:
All 3 confirmed bugs fixed (StrictMode throw, sync↔async trampoline clobber, Remove-Module resurrection), plus the dead-code guard resolved itself as a side effect. Two lower-severity items (global-flag placement doc, test asserts string-equality not behavior) left as-is — flagged but not fixed, your call if you want those too.

Build, lint, unit tests, and 12 live PowerShell scenarios (original wrapper-survival/blank-first-draw/re-init/removal, plus StrictMode, sync→async switch, and falsy-original removal) all pass. Squashed into `9f012815` via fixup+autosquash.
